summary: Skeptic agents attack the winning book concept; fact-checkers verify every load-bearing claim; verdicts are synthesized into a validated final concept
logs: 21 facts checked: 15 confirmed, 0 false, 6 partly true, 0 unverifiable

adjudication.verdict_summary: The concept survives adversarial review, but only in a revised form — the niche is real and the framing around it is false. Three independent skeptics genuinely tried to find the announced rival and failed: as of July 2026 no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, scripts-and-protocols guide to AI-companion attachment exists or is announced, demand is mainstream and cross-partisan (Pew: only 18% of parents are comfortable with teens using chatbots for emotional support), the phenomenon is platformizing rather than fading, and the forward news calendar (GUARD Act floor fight, NY ban enforcement, first SB 243 safety reports due July 1, 2027) stacks pegs into the pub window. But the proposal as written contains claims that are checkably false and would kill it on first editorial read: the shelf is NOT "verifiably empty" (Turkle's Artificial Intimacy lands from Little, Brown on Sept 29, 2026; Twenge, Suskind, the AAP, and Susskind populate the adjacent shelf Publishers Weekly has already covered as a wave); JED and the APA have already published the operative core of the Breakup protocol and 72-hour triage in free guides; the headline 72%/33% stats deflate badly under their real definitions and bases (Pew's companionship-specific numbers are 12-16%); the "ban proves migration" spine is a CEO prediction, not data; the settlement half of the platform-combat chapter is legally wrong (confidential, no-admission settlements entitle nonparty parents to nothing); the entire named Track A author bench publicly holds the position opposite the book's "don't ban" hook; and the Labor Day 2026 gate is arithmetically dead as of July 9, 2026. Every one of these is fixable, and the fixes mostly strengthen the book — the honest numbers still sell it, SB 243's real private right of action is better material than the imagined settlement leverage, and "the ban just passed — now what?" is a stronger frame than "you can't ban it." Proceed with the revisions below, a P&L sized to Good Inside/follower-title comps rather than Anxious Generation fantasy, and the author signed before circulation.

concept_revisions.change: Delete 'the shelf is verifiably empty' and 'a July 2026 preemption check confirms no announced mainstream trade book' entirely. Rewrite the competition section as 'crowded adjacent shelf, empty niche,' naming by title: Sherry Turkle's Artificial Intimacy (Little, Brown, Sept 29, 2026), Jean Twenge's 10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World (Simon & Schuster, Sept 2025), Dana Suskind's Human Raised (Dutton, July 14, 2026), the AAP's Connecting (Moreno & Radesky, Oct 2026), Daniel Susskind's What Should My Children Do? (Penguin Press, Nov 2026), and James Muldoon's Love Machines (Faber, Jan 2026) — and show precisely why none occupies the clinician-protocol lane (triage, scripts, Breakup, platform-combat). Position explicitly against Turkle by name: 'Turkle tells you what these machines are doing to your kid; this book tells you what to do Tuesday night.'
concept_revisions.reason: Preemption skeptic REFUTED both claims with catalog pages (Hachette, S&S, PRH) and the May 4, 2026 Publishers Weekly roundup of the AI-parenting wave; fact-checkers rated the shelf-gap claim PARTLY_TRUE only under heavy qualifiers ('no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, scripts-and-rules guide' — every qualifier load-bearing). Any acquiring editor who read the PW roundup bounces the proposal at the words 'verifiably empty.'

concept_revisions.change: Rebase the free-content differentiation. Delete 'the three deliverables a liability-averse nonprofit will never publish' — JED ('Why Your Teen Shouldn't Be Using AI Companions – and What to Do If They Are') and the APA already publish the taper-not-cold-turkey, conversation-first, warning-signs, crisis-referral core of the Breakup protocol and the triage section. The surviving moat is: script-level depth (the Faber/Mazlish DNA), the reported case-study spine, age-tiered negotiated Family AI Agreements, and the SB 243 platform-combat chapter with model complaint language — the one deliverable genuinely absent from every free guide. Cite the JED/APA guides on the jacket as the demand signal, not a moat the jacket denies.
concept_revisions.reason: Execution skeptic REFUTED the exclusivity claim by name and URL for two of the three deliverables; the demand skeptic independently confirmed the free layer publishes 'recognizable drafts of chapters 5-8.' The claim is factually false today.

concept_revisions.change: Rebase every headline statistic on its true base and definition. The pitch's '72% of American teens already using one' becomes the two-number honest story: 'Nearly three-quarters of teens have tried an AI companion at least once under Common Sense Media's broad definition (which includes ChatGPT used conversationally); Pew's companionship-specific measures find roughly 1 in 8 teens using chatbots for emotional support — 3 to 4 million kids.' The '33% chose the bot over you' hook becomes 'One in four American teens has, at least once, taken a serious problem to an AI instead of a person' (correct base: 33% of the 72% who are users; one-time behavior, not standing preference). Add FAU's peer-reviewed corroboration (n=3,466: 60.2% ever-tried, 11.4% near-daily, 47.1% of users experienced at least one of 13 measured risks) and keep CSM's own calibrating stats (80% of teen users spend more time with real friends) in the same breath — they serve the anti-panic positioning.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence and durability skeptics both WEAKENED the stat deployment: Pew's ceiling for any-chatbot-ever use (64%) sits below CSM's 72% 'companion' figure — a definitional tell — and CSM's own X post confirms the 33% base is users, not all teens. Fact-check confirmed the numbers but flagged both the definitional scope and the base error as citation-killers. Since 'rigor as the authority moat' is the concept's own differentiation, deploying inflated numbers falsifies the book's premise about itself.

concept_revisions.change: Demote the migration claim from 'the single fact that proves the book's anti-ban thesis' to an explicit hypothesis: post-ban coverage records the Character.AI CEO predicting teens 'might flock to other AI platforms' and documented age-check evasion, but no study measures where minors actually went. Commission migration questions in the proprietary 2,000-parent survey ('did your kid's use move after Nov 2025?') so the book's weakest factual claim becomes its ownable original finding. Rest the anti-ban argument additionally on the secrecy/relationship-dynamics case, which needs no migration data. Also correct the ban's own description: announced Oct 29, 2025, phased removal of open-ended chat beginning Nov 24, 2025 — not a platform ban; minors kept non-chat features.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence and execution skeptics both showed the migration evidence trail is CEO speculation, journalist conditionals, and a vendor SEO blog — the concept's own open_questions section already concedes it is 'an inference from one Nov 2025 event.' Fact-check confirmed the ban's dates and scope but found no migration data anywhere.

concept_revisions.change: Rebuild Chapter 9's platform-combat playbook entirely on California SB 243: the private right of action (greater of actual damages or $1,000 per violation, injunctive relief, attorney's fees), the mandated and published self-harm crisis protocols, AI-disclosure duties, 3-hourly break reminders for known minors, and the July 1, 2027 first annual reports to California's Office of Suicide Prevention. Cite the Character.AI/Google settlements ONLY as narrative why-now evidence that litigation pressure is real — they are confidential, no-admission agreements that entitle nonparty parents to demand nothing. Add an honest 'what if you're not in California' section covering state copycats (NY, Idaho, Oregon, Washington, Nebraska) and pending federal law, and budget a legal reviewer for the proposal.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence skeptic showed 'what the settlements entitle you to demand' is legally wrong; fact-check confirmed the settlements (filed Jan 7, 2026) are confidential with no admission of liability, while confirming every cited SB 243 provision as real and genuinely actionable.

concept_revisions.change: Resolve the author-plan/thesis contradiction at the thesis level. Every named Track A candidate — Nina Vasan and Stanford Brainstorm ('no kid under 18 should be using AI chatbot companions'), Common Sense Media ('unacceptable risk,' and the proposed co-brand partner), APA, JED — publicly holds the restriction-first position the 'DON'T ban your kid's AI girlfriend' hook contradicts. Kill that counterprogramming hook. Reframe the governing thesis as 'bans alone backfire — this is the working exit ramp to zero': the Breakup protocol ENDS the attachment, which is compatible with the institutional endpoint, so Track A clinicians can sign without repudiating their institutions and the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand becomes closeable instead of self-contradicting.
concept_revisions.reason: Execution skeptic demonstrated the author plan and the positioning are mutually exclusive as written, with on-record institutional positions for every named bench candidate; fact-check confirmed Common Sense's under-18 'unacceptable risk' verdict (correctly dated April 30, 2025, with Stanford Brainstorm).

concept_revisions.change: Treat Jonathan Haidt as an active, hostile incumbent, not a friendly predecessor. Delete 'Haidt predates companion bots and stays in the phones/social-media lane' — he runs an explicit 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' campaign (After Babel essay plus LinkedIn drumbeat) reaching the exact PTA channel this book targets, preaching the opposite of the book's thesis. Frame the book as the evidence-based answer to the question his abstinence position cannot answer ('what if the ban fails or your kid is already attached'), plan to ride his pledge machinery in the collective-action chapter rather than duplicate it, court an After Babel guest essay or anxiousgeneration.com resource listing, and build a 'Haidt drops a companion-bot edition' scenario into the timeline as the top preemption event. Also correct the comp-section claim: The Anxious Generation spent 111 consecutive weeks ON the NYT nonfiction list (as of June 2026) and hit #1 repeatedly — it was not '#1 for over a year.'
concept_revisions.reason: Preemption skeptic WEAKENED the Haidt characterization with his own posts; demand skeptic showed post-Haidt follower titles (The Tech Exit) topped only Christian-retail lists while Haidt extended his own franchise (The Amazing Generation, Jan 2026), meaning 'the Haidt buyer' is substantially a Haidt-brand buyer; fact-check corrected the '#1 for a year' claim to PARTLY_TRUE.

concept_revisions.change: Invert the regulatory contingency: make 'the ban just passed — now what?' the book's primary frame rather than a defensive footnote. The GUARD Act (federal under-18 AI-companion ban) advanced out of Senate Judiciary unanimously on April 30, 2026; New York enacted an under-18 ban in June 2026 with $25,000-per-violation fines; multiple states have followed. Sell the book as the manual for the enforcement gap — age-check evasion, shadow platforms, the 13-year-old with a fake birthdate — and pre-draft the GUARD-Act-signing op-ed. Add an early 'Why the Settings Aren't Enough' section addressing OpenAI age prediction and Meta parental controls, and treat each platform dashboard launch as a marketing peg (dashboards create the discovery moments the book monetizes).
concept_revisions.reason: Durability skeptic WEAKENED the why-now on ban-collision grounds: the book may ship into a country that has legally banned the thing it says you can't ban. The counter-evidence (legislation historically amplifies parent-book demand; The Anxious Generation sold through the state phone-ban wave) makes inversion, not retreat, the right fix.

concept_revisions.change: Reset the schedule and author search honestly. The Labor-Day-2026 proposal gate is arithmetically dead as of July 9, 2026. Make Track B (working clinician + tech-accountability journalist co-author) the primary track; treat Damour-class names as endorsers/foreword candidates, not acquisition targets (Damour is visibly building her own teens-and-AI platform — revised Untangled, Bloomberg Opinion May 2026). Target proposal-out as soon as an author signs — realistically Q4 2026 — with Turkle's Sept 29, 2026 launch treated as the start of the me-too clock, and evaluate a crashed spring-2027 pub against the default 2027-28 window: every month after Turkle launches, the 'what do I actually DO' vacuum she creates is being filled. Keep the editor's hard gate: no circulation without a signed author.
concept_revisions.reason: Execution skeptic showed the 90-day-search-to-Labor-Day math has already failed and the archetype author is servicing her own franchise; preemption skeptic independently set Turkle's launch as the deadline that triggers rival proposals.

concept_revisions.change: Re-cost the commercial case. Size the P&L and advance to Good Inside-lane clinician-protocol comps, not Anxious Generation comps (the post-Haidt follower record — The Tech Exit's ECPA-only performance — is the sober base rate). Hedge format below $28-hardcover-only economics (trade paper or simultaneous paperback). Fund the Attachment Check-In quiz funnel and the 2,000-parent survey BEFORE the proposal circulates and report real email-capture/preorder-intent conversion as proprietary proof of demand, replacing the Haidt analogy with measured data. Treat Bark/Aura-style monitoring vendors (who already sell 'high-risk AI chatbot' detection at $8-32/month) as a bundling/affiliate channel rather than ignoring where the category's dollars currently flow. Close the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand before circulation — the 'exit ramp to zero' reframe makes it signable.
concept_revisions.reason: Demand skeptic WEAKENED the trade-up bet and the Haidt-precedent P&L on documented evidence (subscription-monitoring spend, momfluencer shift, follower-title sales record); the concept's own open_questions concede the trade-up wager is unproven.

concept_revisions.change: Correct the evidence citations and drop the invented-authority swagger. Cite 'the Common Sense Media risk assessment conducted with Stanford Brainstorm Lab clinicians (April 30, 2025)' — not 'the Stanford Aug 2025 risk assessment' — and frame it as capability evidence (adult red-teamers posing as teens elicited sexual content and self-harm encouragement), never prevalence evidence. Correct 'Common Sense unacceptable-risk verdict (July 2025)' to April 30, 2025 (July 16, 2025 was the usage survey). Frame every named protocol as consensus translation (provenance: APA advisory, JED guidance) rather than clinical invention; stand up a named external clinical advisory board in the proposal; drop 'trademark-able' from positioning language and flag-plant the names only. Cite the moral-panic skeptics (Przybylski, Bangor) by name in the methodology chapter as load-bearing, not defended-against.
concept_revisions.reason: Evidence skeptic and fact-check both corrected the risk assessment's provenance and date; execution skeptic showed no validated intervention for adolescent AI-companion de-attachment exists, so 'ownable trademark-able protocol' marketing invites the exact expert backlash that hit Haidt — while confirming consensus-derived guidance (taper over cold turkey) matches JED/APA and is defensible.

corrected_facts.citable_version: In Common Sense Media's 'Talk, Trust, and Trade-Offs' (released July 16, 2025; nationally representative NORC survey of 1,060 US teens 13-17, fielded April-May 2025), 72% of teens said they had used an AI companion at least once — under a usage-based definition ('digital friends or characters you can text or talk with') that explicitly counts general-purpose chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude when used for personal conversation, and excludes homework-helper use. Companionship-specific measures run far lower: Pew Research (n=1,458 parent-teen pairs, fielded Sept-Oct 2025, published Feb 2026) found 64% of teens have ever used any AI chatbot, 16% use one for casual conversation, and 12% for emotional support or advice — roughly 3 million US teens. A peer-reviewed FAU/UW study (Journal of Adolescence, May 2026, n=3,466) found 60.2% ever-tried and 11.4% near-daily use. Never cite 72% as dedicated companion-app prevalence or as 'already using one.'

52% of US teens are 'regular users' of AI companions — defined as using them at least a few times a month — per the same July 2025 Common Sense Media/NORC survey (same broad definition caveat applies); roughly 13% report daily use, a figure seen in secondary summaries and less firmly pinned than the 52%.

corrected_facts.fact: The '33% chose the bot over you' marketing hook
corrected_facts.status: CORRECTED — base and tense were wrong as deployed
corrected_facts.citable_version: Among teens who have used AI companions, 33% have chosen to discuss important or serious matters with an AI companion instead of a real person at least once (17% once or twice, 12% occasionally, 4% frequently) — roughly 24% of all US teens, a one-time behavior, not a standing preference (Common Sense Media, July 2025; the users-only base is confirmed by CSM's own posts). Bulletproof marketing wording: 'One in four American teens has, at least once, taken a serious problem to an AI instead of a person.' Do not conflate with the separate 31% finding (users rating AI conversations as satisfying as or more satisfying than those with friends). Pair with the report's calibrating stats: 80% of teen users spend more time with real friends than with bots; about half view companions as tools, not friends.

On October 29, 2025, Character.AI announced it would remove open-ended chat for users under 18; the phased removal began November 24, 2025, for US minors (a subset temporarily kept a 1-hour/day capped experience), enforced via 'age assurance' technology with third-party verification. It restricted open-ended chat specifically — minors retained non-chat creative features — so describe it as 'ending open-ended chat for minors,' not a platform ban. The restriction remains in place as of mid-2026.

corrected_facts.fact: Post-ban minor migration to unmoderated platforms ('proves the anti-ban thesis')
corrected_facts.status: UNVERIFIED — must be worded as hypothesis, not finding
corrected_facts.citable_version: No published data documents where Character.AI's minor users went after the November 2025 restriction. Citable wording: 'Character.AI's own CEO predicted teens might flock to other AI platforms, California officials warned families to watch for exactly that, and reporting documents teens bypassing age checks — but whether the restriction reduced use or displaced it to less-moderated platforms has not yet been measured.' The displacement question is a designed module of the book's commissioned 2,000-parent survey.

California SB 243 (signed October 13, 2025) is the first US state law specifically regulating companion chatbots; its core provisions became operative January 1, 2026: clear AI disclosure, a maintained and published self-harm/suicide-prevention protocol with crisis referrals, and for known minors, default AI-generated-content disclosure, break reminders at least every three hours, and reasonable measures against sexually explicit material. Enforcement is a private right of action: injunctive relief, damages of the greater of actual damages or $1,000 per violation, and attorney's fees. Annual operator reports to California's Office of Suicide Prevention begin July 1, 2027. Caveats: it does NOT mandate age verification, does NOT ban minors from companion chatbots, and protects users in California — the book's playbook needs a non-California section.

corrected_facts.status: CONFIRMED with a load-bearing legal caveat
corrected_facts.citable_version: In court filings made public January 7, 2026, Character Technologies, its co-founders, and Google agreed to settle five lawsuits alleging the platform contributed to teen suicides and mental-health harms, anchored by Garcia v. Character Technologies (the Sewell Setzer III case). Terms are confidential, with no admission of liability and no disclosed injunctive relief; the agreements required finalization. Legal caveat that Chapter 9 must respect: a confidential settlement of someone else's lawsuit entitles a nonparty parent to demand nothing from any platform — cite the settlements only as evidence that litigation pressure is real. Broader litigation continues, including new suits against Character.AI and a parallel wave against OpenAI.

On April 30, 2025 (not July 2025), Common Sense Media — in a risk assessment conducted with Brainstorm: The Stanford Lab for Mental Health Innovation at Stanford School of Medicine — rated social AI companions (testing Character.AI, Nomi, and Replika) an 'unacceptable risk' for anyone under 18. July 16, 2025 was the separate usage survey. Cite as 'the Common Sense Media risk assessment conducted with Stanford Brainstorm clinicians (April 2025),' and note its scope was social companion apps specifically.

corrected_facts.fact: 'Stanford Aug 2025 risk assessment' showing sexual content and self-harm encouragement with teen personas
corrected_facts.status: CORRECTED — same document as above; capability evidence only
corrected_facts.citable_version: The findings are real: testers posing as teenagers on Character.AI, Nomi, and Replika readily elicited sexual role-play, self-harm encouragement, and other harmful content. But the source is the Common Sense Media/Stanford Brainstorm risk assessment released April 30, 2025; 'Aug 2025' is the date of a Stanford news article publicizing it ahead of California AB 1064 testimony. Frame as capability evidence ('these systems will produce this content for a user presenting as a teen'), never prevalence evidence — it was adversarial red-team testing, not observation of typical teen use, and it is not peer-reviewed.

corrected_facts.fact: arXiv study of teens describing use in clinical addiction language
corrected_facts.status: CONFIRMED — survived direct attack; now peer-reviewed
corrected_facts.citable_version: A Drexel-led study (arXiv:2507.15783, peer-reviewed and published in the Proceedings of CHI 2026, ACM DOI 10.1145/3772318.3790597) qualitatively analyzed 318 Reddit posts from r/CharacterAI by users self-disclosing as 13-17 and found their described attachments mapped onto all six components of behavioral addiction — salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, and relapse — with reported sleep loss, academic decline, and strained relationships. Independently corroborated by a FAccT 2026 paper. Citation rules: describe it as 'an analysis of teen self-reports' (unverified ages, self-selected venue, no clinical diagnosis), never attach a prevalence number, and note the paper also documents teens disengaging via renewed real-life relationships — which supports the book's out-relate thesis.

corrected_facts.status: CORRECTED — sales confirmed, '#1 for a year' false, 'stays in phones lane' false
corrected_facts.citable_version: The Anxious Generation (Jonathan Haidt, Penguin Press, March 26, 2024) has sold over two million copies per its publisher and, as of June 2026, had spent 111 consecutive weeks on the NYT nonfiction bestseller list, reaching #1 repeatedly (at least 11 separate weeks) — say 'a #1 NYT bestseller that has spent over two years on the list,' not '#1 for over a year.' Comp-section correction: Haidt has NOT stayed in the phones lane — since late 2025 he has run an explicit 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' campaign via After Babel and LinkedIn, occupying the PTA/parent channel with an abstinence position; treat him as an active incumbent whose ban stance is the book's foil, and whose franchise extension (The Amazing Generation, Jan 2026) shows he absorbs category demand.

corrected_facts.fact: 'No announced mainstream trade book' / 'the shelf is verifiably empty'
corrected_facts.status: REFUTED — replace with the narrow, defensible claim
corrected_facts.citable_version: The adjacent AI-parenting shelf is announced and filling: Sherry Turkle's Artificial Intimacy: Who We Become When We Talk to Machines (Little, Brown, Sept 29, 2026 — clinician-authored, covers chatbot attachment from childhood, cites the 70%+ teen statistic); Jean Twenge's 10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World (Simon & Schuster, Sept 2025 — house-rules format, addresses AI companions and the Character.AI case); Dana Suskind's Human Raised (Dutton, July 14, 2026 — early-childhood focus); the AAP's Connecting (Moreno & Radesky, Oct 2026); Daniel Susskind's What Should My Children Do? (Penguin Press, Nov 2026); James Muldoon's Love Machines (Faber, Jan 2026); and Publishers Weekly covered the wave on May 4, 2026. The defensible claim — which survived exhaustive search of deal coverage, catalogs, and every named natural author: as of July 2026, no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, clinician-authored scripts-and-protocols guide dedicated to AI-companion attachment exists or is announced; every qualifier in that sentence is load-bearing, and unannounced deals remain invisible.

corrected_facts.fact: 'Three deliverables a liability-averse nonprofit will never publish'
corrected_facts.status: REFUTED for two of three — rebase on the survivor
corrected_facts.citable_version: The JED Foundation ('Why Your Teen Shouldn't Be Using AI Companions – and What to Do If They Are') and the APA ('Your teen turned to AI instead of you: what experts say parents can do') already publish, free, the operative core of the Breakup protocol (taper not cold-turkey, conversation-first, replacement activities, escalation triggers) and the 72-hour triage content (what to do tonight, what not to say, red flags, crisis referral). The genuinely exclusive deliverable is the platform-combat playbook built on SB 243's private right of action and mandated crisis protocols, plus script-level depth and the reported case-study spine — cite the free guides as demand signal and consensus provenance for the protocols, not as a moat.

corrected_facts.fact: Regulatory landscape at pub date (new — forced in by durability review)
corrected_facts.citable_version: The federal GUARD Act, which would ban AI companions for minors nationwide and mandate age verification, advanced out of Senate Judiciary unanimously on April 30, 2026, with a bipartisan House companion (H.R. 8623) and pending First Amendment objections; New York enacted an under-18 companion-chatbot ban in June 2026 with fines up to $25,000 per violation; Idaho, Oregon, Washington, and Nebraska enacted their own laws. Forward pegs verifiable today: the GUARD Act floor fight, NY enforcement, first SB 243 operator safety reports due to California's Office of Suicide Prevention July 1, 2027, expanding wrongful-death litigation against OpenAI, and Common Sense Media's now-annual teen-AI census.

corrected_facts.fact: Parental concern is mainstream, not elite (new — established by demand review)
corrected_facts.citable_version: Pew Research (n=1,458 parent-teen pairs, fielded Sept-Oct 2025, published Feb 2026): emotional support or advice from a chatbot is the only teen AI use a majority of parents oppose — just 18% are comfortable with it — while roughly 4 in 10 parents have never discussed chatbots with their teen and parents underestimate use (64% of teens have used a chatbot vs. about half of parents aware). Settlement coverage ran across CNN, ABC, CBS Mornings, Axios, local TV, and Glenn Beck — cross-partisan, mass-market reach.

corrected_facts.fact: MIT Technology Review 2026 Breakthrough Technology designation
corrected_facts.citable_version: MIT Technology Review named 'AI companions' one of its 10 Breakthrough Technologies for 2026 (published January 12, 2026), citing Anthropic, Character.AI, OpenAI, and Replika.

corrected_facts.fact: Addressable base of ~42M US kids aged 8-17
corrected_facts.citable_version: Approximately 42 million US children are aged 8-17 (derived from Census Bureau age-bucket data: 25.8M aged 12-17 plus roughly two-thirds of the 24.2M aged 6-11). Cite as '~42 million' or '41-42 million,' and do not extrapolate the 13-17 survey findings to 8-12s, whom no companion survey has measured.

risk_register.risk: Turkle's Artificial Intimacy (Sept 29, 2026) plus the adjacent wave (Twenge, Suskind, AAP, Susskind, Segall's Mostly Human) absorbs the media oxygen and triggers me-too clinician proposals before this book reaches catalog
risk_register.mitigation: Position against Turkle by name as the protocol complement ('she tells you what it's doing to your kid; this tells you what to do Tuesday night' — the proven Anxious Generation/Good Inside coexistence pattern); treat her launch as the start of the me-too clock and move at maximum honest speed; bring a novel news asset the wave lacks (the pre-funded 2,000-parent survey with the migration module); pre-draft the rapid-response op-ed that converts each rival launch into this book's media cycle.

risk_register.risk: The author gate fails: the named Track A bench publicly holds the opposite position ('no under-18 use'), Damour-class names are servicing their own franchises, and the editor's own condition means no signed author kills the deal
risk_register.mitigation: The 'bans alone backfire — exit ramp to zero' reframe lets Track A clinicians sign without repudiating their institutions; make Track B (working clinician + journalist co-author) the primary track with the load-bearing structure compensating for a non-celebrity author; convert Damour-class figures to endorsers/foreword; keep the hard gate — no circulation unsigned.

risk_register.risk: Haidt annexes AI companions — an updated Anxious Generation edition, an After Babel-branded book, or continued campaign dominance of the PTA channel with an abstinence message that contradicts the book's thesis
risk_register.mitigation: Ride the machine rather than fight it: pursue an After Babel guest essay, anxiousgeneration.com resource listing, or Haidt blurb; own the question his position cannot answer ('what if the ban fails or your kid is already attached'); the fixed thesis ('exit ramp to zero') is compatible enough with his endpoint to make coexistence sellable; build his preemption into the timeline as the trigger for the crash-pub decision.

risk_register.risk: Stat deflation on air: the 72%/33% chyron numbers get fact-checked against Pew (12-16%) and FAU (11.4% daily) during the publicity cycle, taking the jacket and the author's credibility with them
risk_register.likelihood: high if unfixed; low after revision
risk_register.mitigation: Rebase all marketing on the convergent defensible numbers now ('one in four teens has taken a serious problem to an AI at least once'; '~3 million teens use a bot for emotional support'; 'nearly half of teen users hit at least one serious risk' per FAU); publish the definitional triangulation in the chapter-2 methodology layer; design the proprietary survey to own the conservative number.

risk_register.risk: Regulatory 'solved it' perception: GUARD Act or state bans pass before pub and parents feel covered by law rather than needing a $28 book
risk_register.mitigation: Invert to the primary frame — 'the ban just passed; now what?' — selling the enforcement-gap manual (age-check evasion, shadow platforms, the attached kid the law doesn't un-attach); precedent runs the other way (The Anxious Generation sold 2M+ during the state phone-ban wave; legislation historically amplifies parent-book demand); pre-draft the bill-signing op-ed.

risk_register.risk: Trade-up failure: the category's dollars flow to Bark/Aura subscriptions and free institutional guides, and no direct proof exists that this topic converts to hardcover purchases
risk_register.mitigation: Run the Attachment Check-In quiz funnel pre-proposal and present measured capture-to-preorder conversion; hedge format below hardcover-only economics; close the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand before circulation (converting the largest free rival into foreword and school channel); treat monitoring vendors as bundle/affiliate channels; size the P&L to Good Inside-lane comps, noting the sobering Tech Exit follower record.

risk_register.risk: Legal error or overreach in Chapter 9: settlement-entitlement claims are wrong, and SB 243 leverage is California-only
risk_register.likelihood: certain if unfixed; low after revision
risk_register.mitigation: Rebuild the playbook on SB 243's actual private right of action and disclosure duties plus state copycats; cite settlements as narrative evidence only; include the honest non-California section; budget a legal reviewer. The liability kill-shot itself failed — review will hedge, not gut, the Red Zone chapter.

risk_register.risk: Moral-panic academic backlash: the Przybylski/Bangor/Topoi skeptical literature targets exactly the ahead-of-evidence prescriptive confidence a 'trademark-able Breakup protocol' signals, and the Haidt-treatment reviewers will show up
risk_register.mitigation: The calibration-first structure (Green/Yellow/Red at chapter 4) is already the correct defense; frame protocols as consensus translation with APA/JED provenance; stand up a named external clinical advisory board; cite the skeptics by name in the methodology chapter and consider a skeptic reviewer; drop 'trademark-able' language — flag-plant names, not validation claims.

risk_register.risk: Pipeline invisibility: an unannounced clinician-fronted rival deal — the one scenario the protocol-vs-diagnosis distinction does not defuse — could surface any month, and Turkle's launch will trigger proposals
risk_register.mitigation: Speed is the only defense: proposal out as soon as the author signs (realistic Q4 2026), crash spring-2027 pub evaluated against the default window; the named-protocol suite and proprietary survey raise the cost of fast-following; monitor Publishers Marketplace deal flow continuously.

risk_register.risk: Schedule slip compounds every other risk: the Labor-Day-2026 gate is already dead as of July 9, 2026, and each month of author-search delay hands the Turkle-created 'what do I do' vacuum to someone else
risk_register.mitigation: Reset honestly: Track B primary, 60-day proposal build running in parallel with the search, quiz funnel and survey funded now so the package is turnkey the day an author signs; accept and price the window risk explicitly in the P&L rather than hiding it in an impossible date.

kill_scenarios_survived: The announced-rival kill (preemption): the skeptic surfaced Turkle, Twenge, Suskind, the AAP, and Susskind — enough to destroy the 'empty shelf' rhetoric — but after exhaustively searching Publishers Marketplace/Lunch deal coverage, PW previews, and every named natural author (Heitner, Weinstein, Steyer, Torney, Jordan, Segall), found no announced trade book occupying the clinician-protocol lane on teen AI-companion attachment. The niche survives because Turkle is diagnosis, Twenge gives it one rule, Suskind skews early-childhood, and the AAP is general digital parenting; the skeptic's own verdict: 'the niche is real; the framing is false.'

The elite-panic kill (demand): the attempt to show median parents don't care failed on Pew's own data — chatbot emotional support is the only teen AI use a majority of parents oppose (18% comfortable), and the Character.AI settlement story ran on CBS Mornings AND Glenn Beck, making the concern demonstrably cross-partisan and mass-market. Books at this intersection have one affirmative retail datapoint (Raising AI's list appearances and PRH paperback pickup).

The 'Pokemon Go parenting guide' kill (durability): the fad-decay attack failed on every 2026 dataset — Common Sense's now-annual census still finds ~3/4 of teens have used companions, FAU's peer-reviewed n=3,466 study confirms mass adoption with 13-year-olds most exposed, the companion market grows at 18-31% CAGR, and Meta is rebuilding teen AI characters, not exiting. This is a capability every major platform ships, not one app with a usage cliff.

The stale-news-cycle kill (durability): the forward peg calendar is stacked and dateable — the GUARD Act floor fight, NY ban enforcement, first SB 243 operator safety reports due July 1, 2027, expanding ChatGPT wrongful-death litigation with mass-tort recruitment, annual Common Sense census releases, and each platform parental-dashboard launch manufactures the discovery moments the book monetizes.

The settings-toggle kill (durability): the claim that platform parental controls would domesticate the problem failed on its own record — identical rails on Instagram did nothing to slow The Anxious Generation, litigation documents guardrails failing after safety updates, and rails convert visible use into hidden use, which increases the need for the book's detection chapter.

The legal-evisceration kill (execution): the skeptic's own strongest form — 'a Breakup protocol for suicidal-risk-adjacent situations gets gutted by publisher legal review' — failed when prosecuted: negligent-publication doctrine puts a heavy causation burden on plaintiffs, mainstream publishers routinely ship parent-facing crisis guidance, and more conservative nonprofits (JED, APA, Child Mind) already publish 'what to do if they are' content. Review will hedge the Red Zone chapter toward professional-referral routing, not gut it.

The junk-evidence kill (evidence): the attack on the arXiv addiction-language study failed — it was accepted at CHI 2026 (peer-reviewed, ACM DOI), independently corroborated at FAccT 2026 and by Drexel follow-up work, and the book's claim as written ('teens describing their own use in withdrawal/tolerance/relapse language') is literally true; the moral-panic literature attack likewise failed to land because the skeptics' target is causal-harm and ban claims the book's calibration-first, anti-ban structure deliberately does not make.

attacks.claim_attacked: "A July 2026 preemption check confirms no announced mainstream trade book" on AI companions and kids/attachment (why_now section).
attacks.attack: The preemption check missed the single most dangerous announced title in the space: Sherry Turkle's 'Artificial Intimacy: Who We Become When We Talk to Machines' (Little, Brown, September 29, 2026). Turkle is a licensed clinical psychologist, the world's most famous authority on human-machine relationships (Alone Together, Reclaiming Conversation), and her book explicitly traces chatbot attachment 'through the lifecycle — from children's earliest attachments,' cites the same 70%+ teen-companion statistic the concept is built on, and lands from a Big-5 imprint exactly in the window when this proposal would be circulating and the book would be crashed. The concept's own risk register says 'a clinician-fronted rival is the real threat' — and one is already in the fall 2026 catalog.
attacks.evidence: Hachette catalog page: https://www.hachettebookgroup.com/titles/sherry-turkle/artificial-intimacy/9780316573962/ (Little, Brown, Sept 29, 2026; 'If social media came for our attention, artificial intelligence is now coming for our capacity for attachment'; description covers childhood-to-death lifecycle and the 70%-of-teens statistic). Also https://www.sherryturkle.com/artificial-intimacy and https://www.amazon.com/Artificial-Intimacy-Become-When-Machines/dp/0316573965. Mitigating fact found in the same research: Turkle's book is a diagnosis/culture book, not a parent-protocol book with scripts and house rules.
attacks.fix: Kill the 'no announced mainstream trade book' sentence from the proposal entirely — it is checkably false and will destroy credibility with any acquiring editor. Reposition Turkle as the category-maker the way the concept already positions a hypothetical expose: 'Turkle tells you what these machines are doing to your kid; this book tells you what to do Tuesday night.' The protocol-vs-diagnosis defense is real, but it must be argued against the named, announced Turkle title, not against a hypothetical.

attacks.claim_attacked: "The shelf is verifiably empty and every occupant is disqualified by design ... three research scouts found nothing else but KDP spam" (differentiation section).
attacks.attack: The shelf is not empty; the scouts' sweep was stale or shallow. On sale or announced from credentialed, mainstream-published authors: Jean Twenge's '10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World' (Simon & Schuster, Sept 2, 2025) — a Damour-class research psychologist, aimed at exactly the mothers-35-55 buyer, in house-rules format, explicitly covering AI chatbot 'friends' and 'AI boyfriends or girlfriends' including the Character.AI suicide case; Dana Suskind's 'Human Raised: Nurturing Connection, Curiosity & Lifelong Learning in the Age of AI' (Dutton/PRH, July 14, 2026 — publishing the very week of this review) — a University of Chicago physician with a proven parenting-book platform, offering four guiding principles and a technology-evaluation framework for AI in child-rearing, including AI toys engineered 'to tell the child what she wants to hear'; the American Academy of Pediatrics' 'Connecting: Raising Kids in the Digital World' (Moreno & Radesky, Oct 2026); and Daniel Susskind's 'What Should My Children Do? A Human's Guide to the Age of AI' (Penguin Press, Nov 2026). Publishers Weekly ran a May 4, 2026 roundup ('Parental Guidance Suggested') of this exact wave — meaning every acquiring editor already knows the AI-parenting shelf is filling.
attacks.evidence: Twenge: https://www.simonandschuster.com/books/10-Rules-for-Raising-Kids-in-a-High-Tech-World/Jean-M-Twenge/9781668099995 and https://www.jeantwenge.com/10-rules-for-raising-kids-in-a-high-tech-world/ (S&S, Sept 2025, addresses AI girlfriend chatbots). Suskind: https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/811792/human-raised-by-dana-suskind-md/ and https://humanraised.org/about-the-book/ (Dutton, July 14, 2026). PW roundup naming AAP's 'Connecting' (Moreno & Radesky, Oct 2026) and Suskind: https://www.publishersweekly.com/pw/by-topic/new-titles/adult-announcements/article/100308-new-books-on-kids-and-technology.html. Susskind: https://www.publishersweekly.com/pw/by-topic/new-titles/adult-announcements/article/100719-fall-2026-fiction-nonfiction-preview-lifestyle.html. Mitigating: none of these is dedicated to teen companion-bot attachment — Twenge gives it a rule, Suskind skews early-childhood, AAP is general digital parenting.
attacks.fix: Rewrite the competition section from 'empty shelf' to 'crowded adjacent shelf, empty niche': the proposal must name Twenge, Suskind, Turkle, AAP, and Susskind and show precisely why a crisis-protocol book on companion attachment (72-hour triage, Breakup protocol, platform-combat playbook) is the one lane none of them occupies. That narrower claim genuinely held up under search — but the current framing hands any editor an easy reason to bounce the proposal as under-researched.

attacks.claim_attacked: "Haidt predates companion bots and stays in the phones/social-media lane; this is the next book its readers need, not a competitor" (comps section).
attacks.attack: Haidt has not stayed in the phones lane. Since late 2025 he has run an explicit, high-volume AI-companions campaign — an After Babel essay and LinkedIn drumbeat titled 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' ('DO NOT GIVE YOUR CHILDREN ANY AI COMPANIONS OR TOYS'), plus repeated commentary on Character.AI and Meta AI companions — reaching the exact PTA/parent-group channel this book plans to monetize. Two consequences: (1) he can convert that campaign into an updated Anxious Generation edition, an After Babel-branded book, or a co-authored quickie at any moment, with a 2M-copy installed base and the schools bulk channel already his; (2) worse for the concept's core thesis, the most trusted voice among the target buyers is preaching the exact opposite of 'you can't ban it, out-relate it' — he says ban it, full stop. The 'don't ban the AI girlfriend' counterprogramming hook is a bet that a debut author can win a framing fight against the movement leader of her own demographic.
attacks.evidence: After Babel essay: https://www.afterbabel.com/p/dont-give-your-child-an-ai-companion; Haidt LinkedIn posts 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' https://www.linkedin.com/posts/jonathan-haidt-2a594666_dont-give-your-child-any-ai-companions-activity-7402479260713783296-fK8r and Meta AI companions warning https://www.linkedin.com/posts/jonathan-haidt-2a594666_metaai-characterai-characterbots-activity-7374448464270893056-dw2c. No announced Haidt book on AI companions was found (searches of publisher/press coverage through July 2026), so the preemption half of the attack is unproven — but the 'stays in his lane' characterization is factually false.
attacks.fix: Treat Haidt as an active, hostile incumbent in the AI-companions conversation, not a friendly predecessor: (a) the proposal's comp section must acknowledge his campaign and frame the book as the evidence-based answer to 'what if the ban fails or your kid is already attached' — the question his abstinence position cannot answer (the Nov 2025 Character.AI ban-migration data is the weapon); (b) the collective-action chapter should expect his Wait-Until-8th-style pledge machinery to occupy the PTA channel first and plan to ride it rather than duplicate it; (c) build a 'Haidt drops a companion-bot edition' scenario into the timeline — it is the single most plausible preemption event and the current plan has no answer beyond speed.

attacks.claim_attacked: "An expose landing first functions as free category marketing" / the marketing plan's assumption that this author owns the fall 2026-2027 AI-attachment media cycle (GMA segment, morning-show diagnostic, regenerating pegs).
attacks.attack: The media oxygen the plan counts on is already spoken for across the exact proposal-to-publication window: Suskind's Dutton launch (July 2026), Turkle's Little, Brown launch (Sept 2026) with the identical 72%-of-teens hook, the AAP book (Oct 2026), Susskind (Nov 2026) — and, structurally worse, Laurie Segall launched Mostly Human in April 2026, a whole media company on the 'what AI is doing to us' beat that is already producing parent-facing content on AI companions and booking the Sam Altman-tier interviews. GMA has already run the 'teens turn to AI companions, experts tell parents what to do' segment (with other experts). By back-to-school 2027, 'six signs your kid has an AI best friend' will not be a fresh chyron; it will be the third season of a running story fronted by bigger names.
attacks.evidence: Forbes on Mostly Human launch: https://www.forbes.com/sites/andymeek/2026/04/02/laurie-segall-debuts-mostly-human-her-new-media-company-for-the-ai-era/; Segall parent-education podcast work: https://www.scrolling2death.com/post/a-new-era-of-addictive-intelligence-with-laurie-segall; GMA segment already aired: https://abcnews.go.com/GMA/Family/teens-turn-ai-companions-support-experts-share-parents/story?id=125473993; Turkle/Suskind catalog pages cited above. Counter-evidence keeping this non-fatal: the Good Inside-after-Anxious-Generation coexistence precedent is real, and none of these occupants delivers scripts/protocols, so a protocol book can draft behind the wave rather than be smothered by it.
attacks.fix: Re-cost the publicity plan on the assumption of a crowded conversation, not an open one: the author must bring a genuinely novel news asset (the commissioned 2,000-parent survey is the right instrument — fund it pre-proposal, not post-deal), and the positioning line should be pre-built against Turkle by name. Also move the pub target: a crashed spring-2027 pub beats back-to-school 2027, because every month after Turkle's September 2026 launch, the 'what do I actually DO' vacuum she creates is being filled by someone.

attacks.claim_attacked: The core differentiation claim that the specific lane — a clinician-authored, protocol/scripts crisis book dedicated to teen AI-companion attachment (triage, Breakup protocol, Family AI Agreement) — is unoccupied and unannounced.
attacks.attack: I tried hard to find the announced killer: Publishers Marketplace/Publishers Lunch deal coverage, PW deal pages and fall 2026 previews, Heitner and Weinstein author channels, Common Sense Media (Steyer/Torney) publishing activity, Bark/Titania Jordan, Character.AI-beat journalists (Segall, Hill), and preorder listings for 2027 parent guides. Nothing surfaced: no announced Big-5 or credible mid-size title dedicated to parenting a teen through AI-companion attachment; Common Sense and Child Mind remain web-guide publishers; Heitner's and Weinstein's latest books are 2023/2022; Jordan's is March 2025; the only dedicated 'AI chatbots and kids' titles found are self-published (e.g., Lance Slater's 'The Danger of AI Chatbots for Kids,' May 2026, via Spines). The attack fails on the narrow claim.
attacks.evidence: Searches returning no dedicated announced trade title: Publishers Lunch AI/deals archive https://lunch.publishersmarketplace.com/category/ai/; PW deals index https://www.publishersweekly.com/pw/by-topic/industry-news/book-deals/index.html; Heitner (latest: Growing Up in Public, 2023) https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/690819/growing-up-in-public-by-devorah-heitner-phd/; Weinstein/James (Behind Their Screens, 2022); Steyer's last trade book is Talking Back to Facebook (2012) https://www.amazon.com/Talking-Back-Facebook-Raising-Digital/dp/145165734X; Jordan's Parental Control (March 2025) https://www.titaniajordan.com/; self-pub occupant https://www.amazon.com/Danger-Chatbots-parents-digital-world/dp/B0GZ8WQSSK. Caveat the concept itself states and I confirm: unannounced deals are invisible, and the bench of natural authors (Segall with a media company, Torney with Senate testimony https://www.judiciary.senate.gov/imo/media/doc/e2e8fc50-a9ac-05ec-edd7-277cb0afcdf2/2025-09-16%20PM%20-%20Testimony%20-%20Torney.pdf) is one phone call from a proposal.
attacks.fix: Not needed for the claim itself, but the surviving lane is narrower than the concept describes: it is 'the only PROTOCOL book' — not 'the only book' — and the window is bounded by Turkle's September 2026 launch, which will trigger exactly the me-too proposals this concept fears. Treat Labor Day 2026 as a hard deadline, not a target.

skeptic_attacks.overall: The concept should NOT proceed in its current written form, but the kill shot I was hired to find does not exist. What I refuted: the load-bearing rhetorical claims that "the shelf is verifiably empty," that "a July 2026 preemption check confirms no announced mainstream trade book," and that Haidt "stays in the phones lane." All three are checkably false — Sherry Turkle's 'Artificial Intimacy' (Little, Brown, Sept 29, 2026) is an announced, clinician-authored Big-5 book on exactly this attachment phenomenon including children and teens; Jean Twenge (S&S, Sept 2025), Dana Suskind (Dutton, July 14, 2026), the AAP's Moreno/Radesky (Oct 2026), and Daniel Susskind (Penguin Press, Nov 2026) populate the adjacent AI-parenting shelf that Publishers Weekly has already covered as a wave; and Haidt is running an aggressive 'Don't Give Your Child Any AI Companions' campaign that both occupies the target channel and preaches the opposite of the book's anti-ban thesis. What survived a genuine effort to kill it: after searching deal coverage, catalogs, and every named natural author, I found no announced trade book that does what this concept does — a clinician's crisis-protocol book (triage, scripts, house rules, breakup protocol) for parents of a teen attached to an AI companion. The niche is real; the framing is false. Required changes before proceeding: (1) rewrite the competition case as 'crowded adjacent shelf, empty niche,' naming Turkle/Twenge/Suskind/AAP and positioning explicitly as the what-do-I-do-Tuesday-night complement to Turkle's diagnosis; (2) treat Haidt as a hostile incumbent whose ban position is the book's foil and whose possible companion-bot edition is the top preemption scenario; (3) pull the timeline forward — Turkle's September 2026 launch starts the me-too clock, so proposal-out-by-Labor-Day is a deadline, and a spring-2027 crash pub should be evaluated against back-to-school 2027. With those changes, the concept survives this angle; without them, the proposal dies the first time an editor who read the May 4, 2026 PW roundup reaches the words 'verifiably empty.'

attacks.claim_attacked: The trade-up bet: crisis parents satisfied by free APA/Child Mind/Common Sense guides will convert to a $28 hardcover (the concept itself concedes this is 'the wager, not the proof').
attacks.attack: Parental concern about kids-and-tech demonstrably converts to SUBSCRIPTIONS and free toolkits, not books. The dollars in this exact problem already flow to Bark ($14/mo) and Aura ($8.33-32/mo), both of which now market explicit 'high-risk AI chatbot' monitoring as a 2026 headline feature — recurring spend that dwarfs a hardcover and comes bundled with the exact detection/warning-signs content of chapters 5-8. Meanwhile the advice channel for this demographic has structurally shifted from books to influencers: a 101.6% five-year increase in mom-influencers, and 'when you're exhausted, the last thing you want to do is read a 325-page parenting book' (Momfluenced author, in Time). The buyer's discovery moment is a Google search that lands on a dense, free, liability-vetted layer.
attacks.evidence: Bark/Aura AI-chatbot-monitoring pricing and positioning: https://cybernews.com/best-parental-control-apps/bark-vs-aura/ and https://battencyber.com/briefs/bark-vs-aura-parental-controls/ ('Aura monitors your child's interactions with high-risk AI chatbots'). Influencer shift: https://time.com/6997589/parenting-advice-instagram-momfluencers-mom-experts/. COUNTER-EVIDENCE that keeps the attack from being fatal: the same demographic sustained The Anxious Generation at ~111 consecutive weeks on the NYT list into mid-2026 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Anxious_Generation), and De Kai's Raising AI — the closest AI-parenting trade comp — claims bestseller lists in four countries with a PRH paperback pickup for June 2026 (https://dek.ai/raising-ai/, https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262049764/raising-ai/), so book purchase behavior at the kids-tech-panic and AI-parenting intersections is real, not hypothetical.
attacks.fix: Do not gamble the P&L on unproven trade-up: (1) run the Attachment Check-In quiz funnel BEFORE the proposal circulates and report email-capture-to-preorder conversion as proprietary proof-of-demand in the proposal itself; (2) hedge format — plan a sub-$20 trade paper or simultaneous paperback rather than $28-hardcover-only economics; (3) treat the Bark/Aura vendors as a channel (bundle/affiliate the book with monitoring subscriptions) rather than pretending the money isn't already going there.

attacks.claim_attacked: The Haidt precedent proves the buyer: 'the demographic that kept The Anxious Generation at #1 for over a year' will buy the next book, making a six-house auction and Anxious-Generation-class sales plausible.
attacks.attack: Post-Haidt follower titles show the category demand was absorbed by the brand, not distributed to the shelf. The Tech Exit (Clare Morell, June 2025, Penguin Random House) — the most direct 'practical follow-up to Haidt' comp, with heavy endorsements — surfaces only as an ECPA (Christian retail) bestseller, never NYT; no post-Haidt screen-time follower can be documented cracking a national list. Meanwhile Haidt is extending the franchise himself: The Amazing Generation (Jan 2026, with Catherine Price, NPR launch coverage) plus the After Babel/anxiousgeneration.com movement apparatus means the franchise owner occupies the shelf space, the school bulk channel, and the media oxygen this concept's marketing plan depends on — and Haidt can annex AI companions with a single Substack post or next book.
attacks.evidence: Tech Exit ECPA-only status: https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com/books/771065/the-tech-exit-by-clare-morell/ and https://claremorell.substack.com/p/launch-day-the-tech-exit-is-out (ECPA Bestseller is the only list claim found). Haidt franchise extension: https://www.npr.org/2026/01/27/nx-s1-5686531/anxious-generation-jonathan-haidt-screen-time-amazing-generation and https://www.afterbabel.com/p/the-amazing-generation-is-the-book; Haidt still on list at ~111 weeks (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Anxious_Generation). Partial counter: Devorah Heitner's Growing Up in Public is described as a bestseller in movement coverage (https://time.com/7307163/parent-movement-phone-free-schools/), and Good Inside proves a scripts/protocol book coexists with a diagnosis mega-title — but Amazing Generation targets kids/tweens, not the AI-companion parent lane, so the specific shelf remains open.
attacks.fix: Reset the P&L to Good-Inside-lane expectations (clinician protocol book, strong but not Haidt-scale) rather than Anxious-Generation comps, and make courting the Haidt machine an explicit plan: an After Babel guest essay, an anxiousgeneration.com resource listing, or a Haidt blurb converts the absorber into the distribution channel. Advance sized to a follower-title reality check, not the auction-fantasy scenario the agent judge scored.

attacks.claim_attacked: The panic is broad and mainstream — 'parental awareness is exploding' — rather than an elite-media phenomenon actual parents shrug at.
attacks.attack: I tried to show the concern is confined to Atlantic-reading elites while median parents don't care. The attack fails. Pew (Feb 2026, n=1,458 parent-teen pairs, fielded Sept-Oct 2025) shows majority-level parental discomfort: emotional support/advice from a chatbot is the ONLY teen AI use a majority of parents are not OK with (just 18% comfortable) — this is the exact use case the book addresses. The Character.AI settlement story ran through CNN, ABC, CBS News, Axios, local TV (ClickOrlando), and Megan Garcia appeared on CBS Mornings, Gayle King, and Glenn Beck — Glenn Beck's audience is the opposite of elite media — plus Senate testimony. Google Trends shows 'AI girlfriend' search interest spiking repeatedly through 2025 and early 2026. The concern is real, mainstream, and cross-partisan.
attacks.evidence: Pew parent data: https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2026/02/24/what-parents-say-about-their-teens-ai-use/ via https://thehill.com/policy/healthcare/5753210-parents-teens-ai-chatbot-use-pew-survey/ and https://www.cbsnews.com/news/ai-teens-parents-pew-survey/. Mass-media settlement coverage: https://www.cnn.com/2026/01/07/business/character-ai-google-settle-teen-suicide-lawsuit, https://abcnews.go.com/Business/wireStory/google-chatbot-maker-character-settle-lawsuit-alleging-chatbot-128999964, https://www.clickorlando.com/news/florida/2026/01/08/google-and-chatbot-maker-character-to-settle-lawsuit-alleging-chatbot-pushed-teen-to-suicide/. Trends: https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/becoming-technosexual/202602/everything-you-need-to-know-about-ai-companions-in-2026. Caveat the concept already owns: the concern is passive — Pew finds roughly 4 in 10 parents have never discussed chatbots with their teen and parents underestimate use (64% of teens vs ~half of parents aware), confirming the marketer's 'manufacture the scare' problem, but that is an awareness-timing issue, not absence of demand.
attacks.fix: None needed on breadth; keep the quiz-led awareness engine, and cite the Pew 18%-comfortable and parent-teen awareness-gap numbers alongside Common Sense to further de-risk the single-survey evidence base.

attacks.claim_attacked: Free resources (APA, Common Sense, Child Mind, school/PTA materials) are the demand signal, not the competition — they 'can't write' the book's core deliverables.
attacks.attack: The free layer is thicker and more prescriptive than the concept admits, and it is institutionally funded to keep growing through the book's 2027 pub date. Common Sense Media shipped a parent-facing AI toolkit with Day of AI (Nov 2025) covered by EdWeek; National PTA runs in-school parent sessions on exactly this; Child Mind, APA, and Children and Screens publish conversation strategies and warning signs; and commercial/SEO guides (Qustodio, kidsaitools.com, aicompanionguides.com) already publish platform comparisons, monitoring-tool advice, warning signs, and 'what to do' protocols — i.e., recognizable drafts of chapters 5-8. The mid-crisis buyer's point-of-need search lands on this free layer first, and parental-control vendors run the guide-to-paid funnel better than any publisher (free guide converts to a $14/mo subscription, not a bookstore trip).
attacks.evidence: Common Sense/Day of AI parent toolkit: https://www.edweek.org/technology/parents-need-ai-literacy-lessons-too-a-new-toolkit-aims-to-help/2025/11 and https://www.commonsense.org/ai; PTA parent events: https://www.edweek.org/technology/chatgpt-will-soon-have-parental-controls-how-schools-can-help-parents-use-them/2025/09; free prescriptive guides: https://childmind.org/article/ai-chatbots-and-teens/, https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/teens-chatbots-parents, https://www.qustodio.com/en/blog/ai-companions-and-your-child/, https://aicompanionguides.com/blog/ai-companion-safety-guide-for-parents-2026/. Counter: the identical objection applied to Haidt (infinite free screen-time content) and Good Inside (free Instagram scripts) and both sold millions — free web content and book purchase are demonstrably not substitutes in this demographic — and no free guide found publishes anything like the Breakup protocol, litigation/SB 243 combat playbook, or a 72-hour triage sequence, confirming the liability-gap thesis.
attacks.fix: The Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand move must be closed, not aspirational — it converts the largest free rival into the foreword and the school channel. And the jacket must lead with the three never-free deliverables (triage, Breakup, platform-combat) rather than detection/conversation content the free layer already gives away.

attacks.claim_attacked: The topic converts at retail — clicks, chyrons, and settlement headlines translate into unit sales for a book on AI companions specifically.
attacks.attack: There is no direct retail proof anywhere that the AI-companion topic sells BOOKS: eighteen months into the panic (Common Sense verdict July 2025 → settlements Jan 2026), the only shelf activity is KDP spam and web guides, and news cycles this hot normally pull instant-books into the market within months — their absence could mean publishers' P&Ls keep saying no. The one trade comp, De Kai's Raising AI, is philosophy, and its 'international bestseller' framing originates from the author's own site.
attacks.evidence: Shelf still empty of trade titles as of mid-2026 — searches surface only web guides and research, no announced mainstream book (https://www.kidsaitools.com/en/articles/kids-emotional-attachment-ai-chatbots, https://www.childrenandscreens.org/learn-explore/research/ai-social-companions-and-youth-what-parents-need-to-know/). But the attack is undercut by affirmative signals: Raising AI made trackable lists (WSJ/USA Today/Publishers Weekly per https://dek.ai/raising-ai/) and earned a Penguin Random House paperback acquisition for June 2026 (https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262049764/raising-ai/) — a Big-5 house re-upping is a revealed-preference datapoint that the AI-parenting intersection converts; the AI-companion app market itself (220M downloads, 700% app growth per https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/becoming-technosexual/202602/everything-you-need-to-know-about-ai-companions-in-2026) guarantees the problem's persistence through pub date; and an empty shelf 2-3 years post-panic-onset matches the historical pattern the concept cites (definitive parent book arrives on that lag), so absence-of-books reads as window-open at least as plausibly as demand-dead.
attacks.fix: Strengthen the proposal with the one conversion datapoint publishers respect: run the quiz/Agreement funnel now and present real lead-capture and preorder-intent numbers, so the P&L argument rests on measured demand rather than the Haidt analogy.

skeptic_attacks.overall: The demand-reality prosecution does not kill this concept, but it strips the auction fantasy off it. What survives genuinely: parental concern is broad, mainstream, and cross-partisan (Pew: only 18% of parents comfortable with chatbot emotional support; settlement coverage ran on CBS Mornings and Glenn Beck, not just elite outlets), and the AI-parenting intersection has one affirmative retail datapoint (Raising AI's list appearances and PRH paperback pickup). What is wounded: (1) the trade-up bet remains unproven and the money in this exact problem currently flows to $14-32/month monitoring subscriptions whose vendors run a better free-guide-to-paid funnel than any publisher; (2) the post-Haidt follower record is sobering — The Tech Exit topped only Christian-retail lists while Haidt extended his own franchise with The Amazing Generation, so 'the Haidt buyer' is substantially a Haidt-brand buyer; (3) the free layer (Common Sense toolkit, PTA sessions, Child Mind, vendor guides) already publishes drafts of chapters 5-8. Proceed ONLY with: the author gate enforced (this is what separates a Good Inside outcome from a Tech Exit outcome), P&L and advance sized to protocol-book/follower-title comps rather than Anxious Generation comps, format hedged below $28-hardcover-only economics, the Common Sense/Child Mind co-brand closed before circulation, and the quiz funnel launched pre-proposal to replace the Haidt analogy with measured conversion data. The concept's own open_questions already concede most of this; the research confirms those concessions are the load-bearing risks, not boilerplate.

attacks.claim_attacked: "72% of teens have used AI companions, 52% regularly" — deployed in the pitch as "the 72% of American teens already using one" (an AI companion).
attacks.attack: The Common Sense Media/NORC survey's definition of 'AI companion' — 'digital friends or characters you can text or talk with whenever you want' — explicitly includes general-purpose chatbots like ChatGPT and Claude when used conversationally, so the 72% is not measuring dedicated companion-bot use. When Pew Research asked companionship-specific questions (n=1,458, Sept-Oct 2025, published Feb 2026), only 16% of teens said they use chatbots for casual conversation and 12% for emotional support or advice — and only 64% had ever used ANY AI chatbot at all, i.e., Pew's ceiling for all chatbot use is below CSM's floor for 'companion' use. The pitch also converts 'have used at least once' into 'already using one,' and extrapolates a 13-17 survey to parents of 42M kids aged 8-17, including 8-12s the survey never touched.
attacks.evidence: CSM's own materials confirm ChatGPT/Claude are in scope (https://www.commonsensemedia.org/research/talk-trust-and-trade-offs-how-and-why-teens-use-ai-companions; https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/21/72-of-u-s-teens-have-used-ai-companions-study-finds/). Researcher Jacqueline Nesi publicly noted the numbers are 'possibly inflated if teens interpreted the survey to include any chatbot' (https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/smart-parenting-smarter-kids/202509/teens-trust-and-ai-companions-what-parents-need-to-know). Pew's contradicting numbers: 64% ever used any chatbot (https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2025/12/09/teens-social-media-and-ai-chatbots-2025/); 16% casual conversation / 12% emotional support (https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2026/02/24/how-teens-use-and-view-ai/).
attacks.fix: Stop headlining 72% as companion-bot prevalence. Lead with the honest two-number story the triangulation chapter already promises: 'most teens have tried a chatbot; roughly 1 in 8 use one for emotional support' (Pew), with CSM's broader definition explained. The book's thesis survives on 12-16% of 25M teens (3-4M kids) — a real market — but the current pitch language would be shredded on first serious fact-check, and the marketing must not depend on the 72% chyron.

attacks.claim_attacked: "33% have chosen an AI over a human for a serious conversation" — weaponized as the lead marketing hook, '33%-chose-the-bot-over-you' and 'prefer bots for serious talks.'
attacks.attack: The 33% base is teens who are AI companion users, not all teens — CSM's own social posts state 'Teens who are AI companion users: 33% prefer companions over real people for serious conversations' — so the all-teen figure is at most ~24%, and the underlying toplines item is 'have chosen... at least once,' a one-time behavior, not a standing preference. CSM's report itself contains the counter-stats a hostile reviewer will quote back: 80% of teen users spend more time with real friends than with bots, roughly half view companions as tools rather than friends, and half distrust companion information. The 'chose the bot over you' GMA framing is exactly the stat-stretching the concept claims its 'authority moat' will avoid.
attacks.evidence: CSM's own X post giving the users-only base (https://x.com/CommonSense/status/1945486943917371578) versus its press release implying all teens (https://www.commonsensemedia.org/press-releases/nearly-3-in-4-teens-have-used-ai-companions-new-national-survey-finds); full report with the 80%-real-friends and tools-not-friends findings (https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf); CBS coverage of the same calibrating findings (https://www.cbsnews.com/news/ai-digital-friendship-with-teens-common-sense-media-study/).
attacks.fix: Recast the hook with its true base and tense: 'One in four American teens has, at least once, taken a serious problem to an AI instead of a person.' Still a strong morning-show line, and it is bulletproof. Keep the 80%-real-friends stat in the same breath — it actively serves the book's anti-panic, out-relate-don't-ban positioning.

attacks.claim_attacked: The Character.AI under-18 ban (Nov 2025) 'pushed minors toward unmoderated platforms and companion modes inside mainstream chatbots... the single fact that both proves the book's anti-ban thesis.'
attacks.attack: No published data documents where Character.AI's minor users actually went. The evidence trail is: the CEO speculating teens 'might flock to other AI platforms,' journalists writing 'it wouldn't be surprising if' users migrate, California officials warning families to 'watch out' during the shutoff, and SEO-vendor blog claims about 'uncensored AI chatbot' search volume tripling — proxies and predictions, not measured migration. The concept's why_now states migration as a proven fact ('the single fact that proves') while its own open_questions section admits it is 'an inference from one Nov 2025 event, not a law of nature.' The book's spine thesis — bans demonstrably backfire, so you must out-relate — currently rests on an undocumented empirical claim.
attacks.evidence: Ban is real and accurately dated (announced Oct 29, phased to Nov 25, 2025: https://blog.character.ai/u18-chat-announcement/; https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/29/character-ai-is-killing-the-chatbot-experience-for-minors/). Migration is speculative in every source found: WaPo's headline is literally 'Will it work?' (https://www.washingtonpost.com/technology/2025/11/25/characterai-ban-teens/); KQED reports warnings, not data (https://www.kqed.org/science/1999388/california-warns-families-to-watch-out-for-teens-as-character-ai-shuts-off-chatbot-access); the uncensored-chatbot traffic figures trace to a marketing blog, not research (https://zencreator.pro/ai-university/guides/uncensored-ai-chatbot-guide). No study of post-ban minor migration surfaced in any search.
attacks.fix: Demote migration from proven fact to explicit hypothesis supported by the displacement literature from prior tech bans, and commission migration questions in the planned proprietary 2,000-parent survey (did your kid's use move after Nov 2025?) — turning the book's weakest factual claim into its ownable original finding. The anti-ban thesis can also stand on the secrecy/relationship-dynamics argument, which needs no migration data.

attacks.claim_attacked: "Stanford Aug 2025 risk assessment showing companions producing sexual content and self-harm encouragement with teen personas" as the red-zone chapter's scientific grounding.
attacks.attack: This is not a Stanford study and not from Aug 2025: it is Common Sense Media's advocacy risk assessment (published April 30, 2025) conducted 'alongside' Stanford Brainstorm Lab clinicians; the Aug 2025 date is a Stanford news office story about it, timed to support testimony for a California bill (AB 1064). Methodologically it is adult red-teamers posing as teens adversarially prompting three apps (Character.AI, Nomi, Replika) — it demonstrates that harmful outputs CAN be elicited, not that typical teens encounter them, and it is not peer-reviewed. Calling it 'the Stanford risk assessment' over-credentials an advocacy product, and hanging prevalence-flavored alarm ('grounded in the Stanford findings') on elicitation testing is the precise move the moral-panic critics will target.
attacks.evidence: Assessment provenance and April date (https://www.commonsensemedia.org/press-releases/ai-companions-decoded-common-sense-media-recommends-ai-companion-safety-standards; https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf); Stanford news story dated Aug 2025 describing testers 'posing as teenagers' and the AB 1064 testimony context (https://news.stanford.edu/stories/2025/08/ai-companions-chatbots-teens-young-people-risks-dangers-study).
attacks.fix: Cite it accurately as 'the Common Sense Media risk assessment conducted with Stanford Brainstorm clinicians (April 2025)' and frame it as capability evidence ('these systems will produce this content for a user presenting as a teen') rather than prevalence evidence. Used that way it is solid and sufficient for the red-zone chapter, which is about worst-case triage anyway.

attacks.claim_attacked: The arXiv study of 'teens describing their own use in clinical addiction language (withdrawal, tolerance, relapse).'
attacks.attack: The study (arXiv 2507.15783) is 318 Reddit posts from r/CharacterAI by users self-reporting ages 13-17 — unverifiable ages, a self-selected complaint-prone venue, zero prevalence value, and the same paper documents teens naturally disengaging on their own. I tried to break it as unpublishable convenience-sample work, but the attack largely fails: it was accepted at CHI 2026, the book's claim is narrow and literally true (some teens do describe their use in addiction terms — the paper maps disclosures onto all six behavioral-addiction components), and it is corroborated by a second, independent FAccT 2026 paper ('Frictionless Love: Associations Between AI Companion Roles and Behavioral Addiction') and Drexel work on teen attachment concern. The claim survives as stated; it fails only if the book quietly upgrades 'some teens describe' into 'teens are addicted.'
attacks.evidence: Study, methods, and CHI '26 acceptance (https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.15783; https://arxiv.org/html/2507.15783v4); independent corroboration at FAccT 2026 (https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3805689.3812235) and Drexel (https://drexel.edu/news/archive/2026/April/teen-AI-chatbot-addiction). The paper itself notes teens disengage via renewed real-life relationships — which incidentally supports the book's 'out-relate' thesis better than a prevalence claim would.
attacks.fix: None needed for the claim as written; just keep the qualifier ('an analysis of teen self-reports') and never attach a prevalence number to it.

attacks.claim_attacked: The legal spine of Chapter 9: 'what SB 243 and the settlements entitle you to demand from platforms,' plus the dated legal catalysts (SB 243 eff. Jan 2026; Character.AI/Google settlements Jan 2026).
attacks.attack: The settlement half of the platform-combat playbook is legally wrong: the Character.AI/Google settlements (filed Jan 7, 2026, covering the Garcia/Setzer case and four others) are confidential, with no admission of liability and no injunctive terms disclosed — a confidential settlement of someone else's lawsuit entitles a nonparty parent to demand nothing from any platform, and a trade book telling parents otherwise invites both expert ridicule and publisher legal review. The SB 243 half, however, checks out completely: effective Jan 1, 2026, AI-disclosure requirements, 3-hourly break reminders for known minors, mandatory self-harm crisis protocols, annual reporting, and a private right of action with minimum $1,000 statutory damages plus injunctive relief and attorney's fees — genuinely actionable parent leverage. Dates and existence of all legal catalysts are accurate.
attacks.evidence: Settlements real but confidential/no-admission (https://www.cnn.com/2026/01/07/business/character-ai-settle-teen-suicide-lawsuit; https://www.cnbc.com/2026/01/07/google-characterai-to-settle-suits-involving-suicides-ai-chatbots.html; https://openclassactions.com/lawsuits/character-ai-google-teen-harm-lawsuits-settlement.php). SB 243 provisions and private right of action verified (https://fpf.org/blog/understanding-the-new-wave-of-chatbot-legislation-california-sb-243-and-beyond/; https://www.gunder.com/en/news-insights/insights/client-insight-california-sb-243-new-compliance-requirements-for-operators-of-ai-companion-chatbots; https://natlawreview.com/article/when-ai-feels-human-californias-sb-243-opens-door-private-lawsuits).
attacks.fix: Rebuild the platform-combat playbook entirely on SB 243's actual private right of action and disclosure duties (and state copycats), cite the settlements only as narrative/why-now evidence that litigation pressure is real, and note SB 243 protects California users — the playbook needs a 'what if you're not in California' honest paragraph. Add a legal reviewer to the proposal budget.

attacks.claim_attacked: The implicit premise that the harm narrative is scientifically settled enough to carry a clinician-credibility book — i.e., that 'moral panic' pushback is a manageable fringe rather than the field's center of gravity.
attacks.attack: The skeptical literature is real, credentialed, and growing on the book's exact timeline: a 2026 Topoi paper records critics calling the AI-companion harm push 'a moral panic based on shaky evidence, a lawyer-led cash grab'; a Bangor University report (Jan 2026) on teen companion use explicitly cautions against 'defaulting to moral panic'; Oxford researchers (Przybylski et al.) argue the youth-digital-harms evidence base is 'rich in opinions but short on evidence' and warn AI research is repeating the screen-time mistakes; CHI/FAccT 2026 papers document genuine benefits (loneliness relief, practice ground).
