But the attack does not kill: the skeptics' target is causal harm claims and ban advocacy — not the book's actual posture, which is anti-ban, calibration-first (Green/Yellow/Red at chapter 4), and protocol-driven. Notably, the Haidt-camp counter-critique of Przybylski (Sigaud/Rausch/Haidt 2026) shows the fight is two-sided and commercially, the contested science did not stop The Anxious Generation. The pushback constrains execution and stat usage; it does not invalidate the concept, and the concept's structure already anticipates it.
attacks.evidence: Moral-panic critique quoted in Topoi (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11245-026-10371-z); Bangor report cautioning against moral panic while documenting relational use (https://www.bangor.ac.uk/news/2026-01-30-new-report-shines-a-light-on-how-teenagers-are-using-ai-companions); Oxford recommendations warning the field is repeating screen-time errors (https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1071110; https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39855239/); live two-sided academic fight (https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/21677026261425910); benefits literature (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/).
attacks.fix: Make the skeptics load-bearing rather than defended-against: cite Przybylski/Bangor by name in the methodology chapter, and consider a skeptic blurb/reviewer. The book's 'don't ban, out-relate' thesis is the one framing both camps can live with — lean into it.

skeptic_attacks.overall: Do not kill the concept, but do not let the current pitch language anywhere near a fact-checker. Verdict pattern: zero fatal, three major-WEAKENED, and the damage is concentrated exactly where the marketing plan lives. The three headline numbers are all softer than deployed: 72% measures 'ever used any chatbot conversationally, ChatGPT included' and is contradicted by Pew's companionship-specific 12-16% (with Pew's 64% any-chatbot-ever figure sitting BELOW CSM's 72% 'companion' figure — a definitional tell); the '33% chose the bot over you' GMA hook is actually 'one-third of users, at least once' (~24% of teens, one-time behavior, not preference) per CSM's own posts; and the anti-ban spine ('the Nov 2025 ban demonstrably pushed minors to darker platforms — the single fact that proves the thesis') is supported by zero migration data, only CEO speculation and vendor SEO blogs — the concept's own open_questions quietly admits this. The legal facts mostly check out (SB 243's provisions and private right of action are real and genuinely useful; settlement dates accurate), but 'what the settlements entitle you to demand' is legally wrong — they're confidential, no-admission, and entitle nonparties to nothing; Chapter 9 must be rebuilt on SB 243 alone. Two attacks failed honestly: the arXiv addiction-language study is CHI 2026-accepted, independently corroborated, and accurately characterized; and the moral-panic pushback (Topoi, Bangor, Oxford/Przybylski), while real and growing, targets causal-harm/ban claims the book deliberately doesn't make — its calibration-first, anti-ban structure is already the correct defense. Conditions to proceed: (1) rewrite every headline stat with true bases and Pew triangulation before the proposal circulates — the honest numbers (3-4M teens using bots for emotional support; 1 in 4 has taken a serious problem to an AI at least once) still sell the book; (2) demote the migration claim to a hypothesis and make it the proprietary survey's marquee original finding; (3) re-anchor the platform-combat playbook on SB 243's actual private right of action with legal review. The evidence base supports a careful book and cannot support the current sizzle — and since 'rigor as the authority moat' is this concept's own stated differentiation, failing these fixes wouldn't just risk embarrassment, it would falsify the book's premise about itself.

attacks.claim_attacked: The why-now claim that the panic window survives to a back-to-school-2027/2028 shelf because bans won't have 'solved' the problem — the migration thesis rests on regulation staying partial (SB 243-style rails, not prohibitions).
attacks.attack: By pub date, the problem may be legally 'handled': the federal GUARD Act — which flatly BANS AI companions for minors nationwide and mandates age verification — advanced out of Senate Judiciary UNANIMOUSLY on April 30, 2026, and New York passed S 9051 in June 2026 prohibiting companion chatbots for under-18s with $25,000-per-violation fines. Idaho, Oregon, Washington, and Nebraska enacted their own laws. A 2028 reader may live in a country where the government already banned the thing the book says you can't ban — making 'you can't ban it, out-relate it' read as pre-ban advice, and letting anxious parents feel covered by law rather than by a $28 hardcover.
attacks.evidence: Senate Judiciary advanced GUARD Act unanimously 4/30/26 (https://rollcall.com/2026/04/30/ban-on-kids-companion-chatbots-advanced-by-senate-committee/, https://www.globalpolicywatch.com/2026/05/senate-judiciary-committee-advances-guard-act-regulating-minor-use-of-ai/); bill text bans minors from AI companions (https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/3062/text); bipartisan House companion H.R.8623 (https://foushee.house.gov/media/press-releases/reps-foushee-moore-introduce-bipartisan-bill-protecting-children-from-ai-companion-chatbots); NY ban June 2026 (https://www.techtimes.com/articles/318339/20260613/new-york-bans-ai-companion-chatbots-kids-unanimous-vote-sets-25000-fines.htm); state law survey (https://www.multistate.us/insider/2026/4/30/state-childrens-online-safety-laws-expand-beyond-social-media-in-2026). BUT: the same coverage flags First Amendment challenges (https://thefulcrum.us/governance-legislation/senate-judiciary-advances-guard-act-ai-companion-ban), unanimous-committee bills die (KOSA precedent), and the documented aftermath of the Character.AI ban is teens bypassing age checks and migrating to unmoderated open-source platforms (https://www.aicerts.ai/news/character-ai-teen-ban-signals-ai-safety-turning-point/) — bans have so far created the exact underground problem the book addresses. The Anxious Generation sold 2M+ copies DURING a wave of state phone-ban laws; legislation historically amplifies rather than satisfies parent-book demand.
attacks.fix: Invert the contingency: make 'the ban just passed — now what?' the book's PRIMARY frame, not a defensive footnote. The jacket and chapter 13 should assume a GUARD-Act-or-copycat world and sell the book as the manual for the enforcement gap (age-check evasion, shadow platforms, the 13-year-old with a fake birthdate). Pre-draft the GUARD-Act-signing op-ed alongside the exposé-response op-ed.

attacks.claim_attacked: The urgency engine — that the 72%/52%/33% Common Sense numbers (plus 'triangulation against Pew') will still power a credible GMA chyron and a 2028 jacket.
attacks.attack: The triangulation the concept promises actively hurts it: the independent surveys it names as corroboration came in FAR below the headline numbers. Pew (Feb 2026, n=1,458, fielded late 2025) found only 12% of teens have used chatbots for emotional support/advice and 16% for casual conversation; the FAU/UW peer-reviewed study (Journal of Adolescence, May 2026, n=3,466 — triple Common Sense's sample) found 60.2% ever-tried a conversational AI and just 11.4% near-daily use. That is not a 'range,' it is a 4-6x definitional gap between an advocacy nonprofit's broad 'AI companion' framing and academic measurement of the actual panic behavior. Meanwhile an academic backlash is already in print — Bangor University's Jan 2026 report explicitly warns against 'defaulting to moral panic,' and a Springer study ('From moral panic to normalization') finds companion users largely indistinguishable from non-users. By 2028, '33% chose the bot over you' gets fact-checked on air, and the book's scariest sentence is its most fragile.
attacks.evidence: Pew Feb 2026: 12% emotional support (https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2026/02/24/how-teens-use-and-view-ai/, https://techcrunch.com/2026/02/25/about-12-of-u-s-teens-turn-to-ai-for-emotional-support-or-advice/); FAU study May 2026: 60.2%/11.4% (https://www.fau.edu/newsdesk/articles/chatbot-ai-study-teens); Bangor anti-moral-panic report (https://www.bangor.ac.uk/news/2026-01-30-new-report-shines-a-light-on-how-teenagers-are-using-ai-companions); normalization study (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02751-7). Partial counter: Common Sense's now-ANNUAL Census still reports ~3/4 of teens using companions (https://www.commonsensemedia.org/research/a-comprehensive-report-on-teens-tweens-and-ai), a June 2026 report puts ~8M young people (nearly 1 in 5) using AI chatbots for mental-health advice (https://www.forthepeople.com/blog/dark-side-ai-companions-lawsuits-over-teen-harm-are-growing/), and FAU found 47.1% of teens experienced at least one of 13 measured risks — so a large, defensible, still-alarming number exists beneath the inflated one.
attacks.fix: Rebase the marketing spine on the convergent conservative numbers now, not at fact-check time: '3 million teens turn to a bot for emotional support' (Pew) and 'nearly half of teen users hit at least one serious risk' (FAU) are chyron-strength AND survive vetting. Demote 72%/33% to sourced-and-caveated context. The proprietary 2,000-parent survey should be designed to measure the low-end behavior precisely, so the book OWNS the defensible number instead of renting the fragile one.

attacks.claim_attacked: The claim that teen companion use is still growing (not plateauing/declining post-Nov-2025) and that this is a durable phenomenon rather than a 2025 fad — the 'Pokemon Go parenting guide' charge.
attacks.attack: If minors' use had peaked with the Character.AI ban and the settlements, the book ships into a shrinking problem. I looked for evidence of decline or fad-decay and could not find it — every 2026 data point shows persistence or growth, and the phenomenon is platformizing (into ChatGPT, Meta, games) rather than fading with any single app.
attacks.evidence: Common Sense launched an ANNUAL 'Census: AI Use by Tweens and Teens (2026)' still finding nearly 3/4 of teens have used companions, half regularly (https://www.commonsensemedia.org/research/a-comprehensive-report-on-teens-tweens-and-ai); FAU May 2026 peer-reviewed confirmation of mass adoption with 13-year-olds most exposed (https://www.fau.edu/newsdesk/articles/chatbot-ai-study-teens); companion market growing at 18-31% CAGR with ~50M global active users (https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/ai-companion-market-report, https://companionrater.com/ai-companion-statistics-2026); post-ban teens documented bypassing age checks or migrating to unmoderated platforms rather than quitting (https://www.aicerts.ai/news/character-ai-teen-ban-signals-ai-safety-turning-point/); Meta is REBUILDING a teen version of AI characters, not exiting (https://techcrunch.com/2026/01/23/meta-pauses-teen-access-to-ai-characters-ahead-of-new-version/); ~8M young Americans using AI for mental-health advice as of June 2026 (https://www.forthepeople.com/blog/dark-side-ai-companions-lawsuits-over-teen-harm-are-growing/). Pokemon Go was one app with a usage cliff; this is a capability every major platform is shipping. The analogy fails on the evidence.
attacks.fix: None required on the growth claim itself; keep the attachment-dynamics-not-apps architecture, which the Meta/OpenAI platformization evidence vindicates.

attacks.claim_attacked: The claim that the news cycle regenerates through 2027-28 ('SB 243 enforcement actions regenerating coverage through 2027') rather than the story being a stale 2025 panic by pub date.
attacks.attack: The settlements (Jan 2026) closed the biggest peg, the C.AI ban is old news by 2028, and four-year-old tech panics usually can't book morning shows. If mid-2026 coverage were already fading, the 2027-28 media plan collapses.
attacks.evidence: The cycle is demonstrably still live and has a stacked forward calendar: NY ban passed June 2026 (https://www.techtimes.com/articles/318339/20260613/new-york-bans-ai-companion-chatbots-kids-unanimous-vote-sets-25000-fines.htm); GUARD Act awaits a full Senate vote — a guaranteed 2026-27 fight (https://thehill.com/policy/technology/5858006-senate-panel-advances-bill-to-curb-ai-chatbot-companions-for-kids/); litigation is EXPANDING beyond Character.AI to ChatGPT 'suicide coach' wrongful-death claims with mass-tort firms now recruiting plaintiffs (https://www.torhoermanlaw.com/ai-lawsuit/, https://www.forthepeople.com/blog/dark-side-ai-companions-lawsuits-over-teen-harm-are-growing/); SB 243 requires operators' first annual safety reports to California's Office of Suicide Prevention by July 1, 2027 — a fresh, dateable data peg landing exactly at the proposed pub window (https://www.sheppardhealthlaw.com/2025/12/articles/state-legislation/california-sb-243-setting-new-standards-for-regulating-and-ensuring-integrity-of-ai-companion-chatbots/); Common Sense's annual census creates a yearly stat cycle; national features still running mid-2026 (https://prospect.org/2026/02/19/ai-chatbot-experiment-americas-children-big-tech-chatgpt-openai/). One caveat found: SB 243's real-world enforcement so far is private-suit and compliance-memo activity, not the splashy AG actions the pitch implies (https://natlawreview.com/article/when-ai-feels-human-californias-sb-243-opens-door-private-lawsuits).
attacks.fix: Swap the soft 'SB 243 enforcement actions' peg for the hard ones now verifiable: the July 1, 2027 first-safety-reports date, the GUARD Act floor fight, and the ChatGPT wrongful-death docket.

attacks.claim_attacked: The implicit claim that the crisis-parent buyer still exists in 2028 — i.e., that platform safety rails (ChatGPT age prediction, Meta parental controls) won't have domesticated the problem into a settings toggle that makes a protocol book feel obsolete.
attacks.attack: Between the deal and the pub date, every mainstream platform will ship parental dashboards: OpenAI rolled out behavioral age prediction (Jan 20, 2026) defaulting uncertain users to an under-18 experience, plus parental controls with distress notifications and blackout hours; Meta pulled teens from AI characters entirely (Jan 2026) and is rolling out controls restricting teens to 'education, sports, hobbies — not romance' with parent topic-insights. A 2028 parent may believe the fix is a checkbox, not a book — the free-and-built-in substitute problem, worse than the free-web-guide problem the concept already patched.
attacks.evidence: OpenAI age prediction and controls (https://openai.com/index/building-towards-age-prediction/, https://openai.com/index/introducing-parental-controls/, https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/01/21/chatgpt-age-prediction-teen-safety/); Meta teen pause and controls (https://techcrunch.com/2026/01/23/meta-pauses-teen-access-to-ai-characters-ahead-of-new-version/, https://about.fb.com/news/2025/10/teen-ai-safety-approach/, https://www.socialmediatoday.com/news/meta-bans-teen-access-to-ai-bot-characters/810440/). But the attack largely fails on its own record: identical rails on Instagram (Teen Accounts, 2024) did nothing to slow The Anxious Generation; ongoing litigation alleges ChatGPT's guardrails failed catastrophically AFTER safety updates (https://www.torhoermanlaw.com/ai-lawsuit/); documented bypass/migration behavior means rails convert visible use into hidden use — which INCREASES the need for the book's detection chapter ('The Quiet Audit'); and Meta's topic-insight dashboards hand parents exactly the discovery moment the book monetizes.
attacks.fix: Add a short 'Why the Settings Aren't Enough' section early (rails fail, kids evade, dashboards create discoveries parents don't know how to act on) and treat platform parental-control rollouts as marketing pegs — each dashboard launch is a news cycle the author narrates.

skeptic_attacks.overall: The concept should proceed — the 'Pokemon Go parenting guide' kill fails on the evidence. As of mid-2026 the phenomenon is platformizing and regulating, not fading: teen use persists at mass scale in every 2026 dataset (Common Sense annual census ~3/4; FAU peer-reviewed 60% ever-tried with 47% experiencing at least one risk; ~8M young people using AI for mental-health advice), litigation is expanding from Character.AI to ChatGPT with mass-tort firms recruiting, and the forward peg calendar (GUARD Act floor fight, NY ban enforcement, first SB 243 safety reports due July 1, 2027) stacks news cycles directly into a 2027-28 pub window. Two attacks genuinely land and demand changes before proposal. First, stat deflation: Pew (12% emotional support) and FAU (11.4% daily) are 4-6x below the Common Sense 72%/52%/33% chyron numbers the whole marketing engine runs on, and an academic anti-moral-panic literature is already in print — the book must rebase its urgency on the convergent conservative numbers (millions of teens, half of users hitting real risks) it can defend on air in 2028, or it hands the fact-checkers its jacket. Second, ban collision: with the GUARD Act out of committee unanimously and New York's under-18 ban enacted June 2026, the book may ship into a country that has legally banned the thing it says you can't ban; the fix is to promote 'the ban just passed — now what?' from a defensive contingency to the primary frame, selling the book as the manual for the enforcement gap the migration evidence already proves exists. With those two repositionings, the window is not merely durable — the regulatory wave and platform-dashboard rollouts actively manufacture the discovery moments and news pegs the book monetizes.

attacks.claim_attacked: The author gate is managed: a '90-day two-track search' can sign a Damour-class clinician from the named bench in time for 'proposal-out-by-Labor-Day 2026.'
attacks.attack: The clock has already failed and the bench is otherwise occupied. Today is July 9, 2026; Labor Day is ~8 weeks away, so a '90-day search' targeting Labor Day either started in April (nothing in the file says it did) or is arithmetically dead — and that's before agent negotiation, conflict checks with the clinician's institution, and collaboration-agreement lawyering, which routinely take a quarter on their own. Worse, the archetype herself is servicing her own franchise: Lisa Damour released a revised edition of Untangled updated for new-tech challenges and is publishing AI-and-teens commentary (Bloomberg Opinion, May 11, 2026, 'Psychologist Lisa Damour Has Advice on Teens and AI') — i.e., the most plausible Damour-class author is actively building the platform to write this exact book for her existing publisher, which is the one rival scenario the concept admits it cannot defuse. The generic claim that 'a book in search of an author' is always a death does NOT hold — book packaging (packager originates concept, attaches author, sells to publisher) is a documented, legitimate acquisition pattern — but a packaged book needs a realistic bench and a realistic clock, and this has neither as written.
attacks.evidence: Damour revised-Untangled and franchise activity: https://drlisadamour.com/resource/the-new-edition-of-untangled-by-dr-lisa-damour/ and https://drlisadamour.com/books/ ; Damour actively commenting on teens+AI without this book: https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-05-11/psychologist-lisa-damour-has-advice-on-teens-and-ai ; no announced Vasan book deal, only her lab role and prior leadership book: https://www.stanfordbrainstorm.com/nina-vasan , https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nina_Vasan ; concept-first packaging is a real pattern (refutes 'known death' framing): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Book_packaging and https://bookendsliterary.com/book-packager-v-agen/
attacks.fix: Reset the schedule honestly: kill the Labor-Day-2026 gate, make Track B (working clinician + tech-accountability journalist co-author) the primary track rather than the fallback, and treat Damour-class names as endorsers/foreword candidates, not acquisition targets. Move target pub to spring 2028 and accept the window risk explicitly in the P&L.

attacks.claim_attacked: Track A of the author search — Stanford Brainstorm clinician-researchers, Common Sense Media research leads, APA task-force members — is a recruitable bench for THIS book, and Common Sense/Child Mind are viable co-brand/foreword partners.
attacks.attack: The named bench holds the publicly staked position OPPOSITE to the book's flag-planted thesis and lead marketing hook. Nina Vasan and the Stanford Brainstorm/Common Sense team are on record: 'no kid under 18 should be using AI chatbot companions.' Common Sense Media's official risk-assessment verdict is that social AI companions are 'unacceptable' for minors and should not be used by anyone under 18 — and Common Sense is the concept's proposed co-brand partner. The APA advisory and JED Foundation align with restriction-first framing. The book's governing reframe is 'you can't ban it, you have to out-relate it,' and its earned-media hook is 'The parenting expert who says DON'T ban your kid's AI girlfriend.' Any clinician signed from Track A must either publicly contradict their institution's flagship recommendation on GMA or force the book to abandon its core differentiation. The co-brand plan asks Common Sense to foreword a book whose jacket copy argues against Common Sense's own headline verdict. The author plan and the positioning are mutually exclusive as written.
attacks.evidence: Vasan/Stanford 'no kid under 18': https://futurism.com/stanford-no-kid-under-18-ai-chatbot-companions and https://news.stanford.edu/stories/2025/08/ai-companions-chatbots-teens-young-people-risks-dangers-study and https://www.kqed.org/news/12038154/kids-talking-ai-companion-chatbots-stanford-researchers-say-thats-bad-idea ; Common Sense 'unacceptable risk / should not be used by minors': https://www.commonsensemedia.org/press-releases/ai-companions-decoded-common-sense-media-recommends-ai-companion-safety-standards ; APA advisory restriction-first posture: https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being ; JED: https://jedfoundation.org/why-your-teen-shouldnt-be-using-ai-companions-and-what-to-do-if-they-are/
attacks.fix: Resolve the contradiction at the thesis level, not the marketing level: the book's actual content (the Breakup protocol) ENDS the attachment, which is compatible with the institutional endpoint. Reframe as 'bans alone backfire — here is the working exit ramp to zero,' kill the 'DON'T ban the AI girlfriend' counterprogramming hook, and pitch Track A clinicians a harm-reduction-to-abstinence book they can sign without repudiating their institutions.

attacks.claim_attacked: Free-content sufficiency is solved because 'the web guides can't write the Breakup protocol, the platform-combat playbook, or the 72-hour triage section — the three deliverables a liability-averse nonprofit will never publish.'
attacks.attack: This is factually false for two of the three deliverables, today, by name. The JED Foundation has already published 'Why Your Teen Shouldn't Be Using AI Companions – and What to Do If They Are,' and the APA has published 'Your teen turned to AI instead of you: what experts say parents can do' — free guides that contain the Breakup protocol's operative core (don't cold-turkey delete the app; conversation first; gradual reduction with replacement activities; warning signs including distress at loss of access; when to escalate to a professional) and the triage section's operative core (what to do tonight, what not to say, red flags, crisis referral). The liability-averse nonprofits are demonstrably NOT too liability-averse to publish 'what to do if they are' crisis guidance — they are publishing it faster than any hardcover can ship. The one deliverable that genuinely survives is the platform-combat playbook: SB 243's private right of action (greater of actual damages or $1,000 per violation, injunctive relief, attorney's fees) and its mandated crisis protocols are real, specific, and absent from the nonprofit guides.
attacks.evidence: JED guide with taper/what-to-do content: https://jedfoundation.org/why-your-teen-shouldnt-be-using-ai-companions-and-what-to-do-if-they-are/ ; APA parent what-to-do guide: https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/teens-chatbots-parents and https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/tips-to-keep-teens-safe ; Children and Screens parent guidance: https://www.childrenandscreens.org/learn-explore/research/ai-social-companions-and-youth-what-parents-need-to-know/ ; SB 243 private right of action substance (supports the one surviving deliverable): https://fpf.org/blog/understanding-the-new-wave-of-chatbot-legislation-california-sb-243-and-beyond/ and https://www.gunder.com/en/news-insights/insights/client-insight-california-sb-243-new-compliance-requirements-for-operators-of-ai-companion-chatbots
attacks.fix: Stop claiming exclusivity the free web has already broken. Rebase differentiation on what a book can genuinely do that a web guide cannot: script-level depth (the Faber/Mazlish DNA), a reported case-study spine, age-tiered negotiated agreements, and the SB 243/settlement platform-combat chapter with model complaint language — and position the JED/APA guides as the demand signal the jacket cites, not a moat the jacket denies.

attacks.claim_attacked: The named protocols (the Breakup, the Quiet Audit, the 72-hour triage) are clinically defensible deliverables a credentialed author can put their license behind — not invented authority.
attacks.attack: There is no validated intervention for de-escalating adolescent AI-companion attachment. The peer-reviewed literature itself says the evidence base is 'emergent and not youth-specific'; intervention research is at the protocol/RCT-registration stage, not the validated-protocol stage. A trademarked, step-by-step 'Breakup protocol' marketed as 'the book's most ownable protocol' — deployed by untrained parents in situations the book's own Red Zone chapter concedes are suicidal-ideation-adjacent — is precisely the ahead-of-evidence prescriptive confidence the expert class now punishes: the Candice Odgers Nature review and the sustained researcher backlash against The Anxious Generation ('not supported by science,' 'moral panic') is the documented playbook, and this book names Haidt as its comp, guaranteeing the same reviewers show up. The attack does not fully kill: consensus-derived clinical guidance ahead of RCTs is how the entire prescriptive-parenting category works (Good Inside), and the book's taper-not-cold-turkey direction matches the existing JED/APA expert consensus, so a careful author can write defensible protocols. But 'ownable/trademark-able protocol' marketing and evidence humility pull in opposite directions, and the concept's own risk list admits no structural choice constrains an author's tone.
attacks.evidence: Evidence 'emergent and not youth-specific,' intervention gaps: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/ (AI companions and adolescent social relationships: benefits, risks, bidirectional influences) ; intervention research still at RCT-protocol stage: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10024210/ ; Odgers Nature critique and researcher backlash precedent against Haidt-style claims: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Anxious_Generation and https://www.platformer.news/anxious-generation-jonathan-haidt-debate-critique/ and https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adr1730 ; expert consensus does support taper-over-cold-turkey direction (limits the attack): https://jedfoundation.org/why-your-teen-shouldnt-be-using-ai-companions-and-what-to-do-if-they-are/
attacks.fix: Frame every protocol as consensus translation, not invention: cite the APA advisory and JED guidance as the protocols' provenance, stand up a named external clinical advisory board in the proposal, publish the protocols' evidence status in the chapter-2 methodology layer, and drop 'trademark-able' from the positioning language — flag-plant the NAMES, not claims of clinical validation.

attacks.claim_attacked: The Character.AI under-18 ban (Nov 2025) 'pushed minors toward unmoderated platforms... the single fact that both PROVES the book's anti-ban thesis' — the empirical spine of the 'out-relate the machine' positioning.
attacks.attack: The migration is a prediction, not a finding. Contemporary coverage of the ban records Character.AI's CEO acknowledging teens 'might flock to other AI platforms' and analysts saying users 'would likely migrate' — the company literally said it HOPED under-18 users would migrate. I found no post-ban measurement (survey, telemetry, follow-up study) documenting where minors actually went or whether aggregate use continued. The concept's own open-questions list quietly concedes this ('an inference from one Nov 2025 event, not a law of nature') while the pitch language upgrades it to 'proves.' A jacket thesis whose central empirical proof is a defendant CEO's pre-ban press statement gets taken apart by the first serious fact-checker, and the anti-ban framing is already the book's most exposed flank given the institutional consensus (attack 2).
attacks.evidence: Migration as prediction/CEO hope, not measured outcome: https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/29/character-ai-is-killing-the-chatbot-experience-for-minors/ and https://fortune.com/2025/10/29/character-ai-ban-children-teens-chatbots-regulatory-pressure-age-verification-online-harms/ and https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/culture-news/character-ai-sued-teens-suicide-banned-minors-chatbots-1235456426/ ; no post-ban migration study surfaced in searches of 2026 coverage.
attacks.fix: Downgrade the claim from 'proves' to 'suggests' until real data exists, and commit in the proposal to sourcing post-ban usage data (the next Common Sense survey wave, or the commissioned 2,000-parent survey with a migration module) so the anti-ban thesis has measured evidence before catalog copy locks.

attacks.claim_attacked: My own kill-angle's strongest form: 'a book giving Breakup protocols for suicidal-risk-adjacent situations would be gutted by legal/medical review' — i.e., the Red Zone chapter and 72-hour triage section cannot survive a Big-5 vetting pass.
attacks.attack: I prosecuted this and it did not land. US negligent-publication doctrine puts a heavy burden on plaintiffs (duty, foreseeability, and — hardest — causation between text and injury), courts have been reluctant to hold publishers liable for how-to content, and standard practice (prominent warnings, 'consult a professional' routing, crisis-line references) is exactly what the genre already ships: mainstream publishers routinely publish parent-facing suicide-prevention and crisis guidance, and nonprofits with far more conservative counsel (JED, APA, Child Mind) already publish 'what to do if they are' crisis content — so a Big-5 legal pass will hedge the chapter, not gut it. The SB 243 platform-combat material is on even firmer ground: the private right of action ($1,000-per-violation floor, injunctive relief, attorney's fees) and mandated self-harm crisis protocols are statutory fact a book can neutrally report. The genuine residual constraint is editorial, not legal: review will force the Red Zone chapter to route red-zone cases to professionals rather than parent scripts, which modestly shrinks the 'no one else can write this' swagger but leaves the chapter intact.
attacks.evidence: Negligent publication doctrine — difficult causation burden, disclaimers' role: https://corporate.findlaw.com/intellectual-property/publisher-liability-incitement-amp-negligent-publication.html ; nonprofits already publishing crisis-adjacent 'what to do' guidance without being gutted: https://jedfoundation.org/why-your-teen-shouldnt-be-using-ai-companions-and-what-to-do-if-they-are/ and https://childmind.org/article/ai-chatbots-and-teens/ ; SB 243 statutory substance for the platform-combat chapter: https://fpf.org/blog/understanding-the-new-wave-of-chatbot-legislation-california-sb-243-and-beyond/ and https://sd18.senate.ca.gov/news/first-nation-ai-chatbot-safeguards-signed-law
attacks.fix: No structural change needed; write the Red Zone chapter with professional-referral routing as the spine (which the concept already gestures at) and have the clinical advisory board sign off on the crisis-content pages specifically.

skeptic_attacks.overall: Do not kill the concept, but do not proceed as packaged — the pitch contains one false load-bearing claim and one internal contradiction, plus a schedule that has already failed. (1) The 'three deliverables no liability-averse nonprofit will publish' moat is factually false: JED and the APA have already published the Breakup protocol's core (taper, don't cold-turkey, conversation-first) and the triage core in free guides; differentiation must be rebased on script-level depth, the reported spine, and the genuinely surviving SB 243 platform-combat chapter. (2) The author plan and the positioning are mutually exclusive: every named Track A clinician and the proposed Common Sense co-brand partner are publicly committed to 'no one under 18 should use AI companions,' the direct opposite of the 'DON'T ban the AI girlfriend' hook — resolve by reframing the thesis as 'bans alone backfire; here is the exit ramp to zero,' which the bench can actually sign. (3) The Labor-Day-2026 proposal gate is arithmetically dead as of July 9, 2026, and the archetype author (Damour) is visibly building her own teens-and-AI platform — reset the clock, make the clinician+journalist pairing the primary track, and accept a spring-2028 pub. (4) The 'ban proves migration' claim is a CEO prediction, not data — downgrade to 'suggests' and buy the data. The liability attack, honestly prosecuted, failed: legal review will hedge the Red Zone chapter, not gut it, and SB 243 gives the platform-combat playbook real statutory substance. A repositioned, re-clocked version of this book is buildable; the version in this file gets its two loudest claims broken in the first serious vetting pass.

checks.claim: 72% of US teens have used AI companions (Common Sense Media 2025 national survey)
checks.corrected_statement: In Common Sense Media's report 'Talk, Trust, and Trade-Offs: How and Why Teens Use AI Companions' (released July 16, 2025; nationally representative survey of 1,060 US teens ages 13-17), 72% of teens said they had used an AI companion at least once.
checks.notes: Consistent across the primary source (Common Sense Media press release and report/toplines PDFs at commonsensemedia.org) and multiple independent outlets (TechCrunch, CNN, CBS News, Axios). Direct full-page fetches of commonsensemedia.org were proxy-blocked (HTTP 403), so figures were verified via search extracts of the press release/report plus independent press coverage that all agree.

checks.claim: 52% of teens use AI companions regularly / at least a few times a month
checks.corrected_statement: 52% of US teens are 'regular users' of AI companions, defined in the report as using them at least a few times a month (about 13% report daily use), per the same July 2025 Common Sense Media survey.
checks.notes: The 'regular user' label and the 'at least a few times a month' operationalization both appear in Common Sense Media's own materials and in secondary coverage. Some outlets round this to 'over half' or 'about half'. The 13% daily-use figure comes from a secondary summary of the report and was seen in only one extract, so treat it as less firmly pinned than the 52%.

checks.claim: 33% (or 31%?) of teens have chosen to discuss serious matters with AI instead of real people
checks.corrected_statement: The correct figure is 33%, not 31% — and the base is AI companion USERS, not all teens: among teens who have used AI companions, 33% have chosen to discuss important or serious matters with an AI companion instead of a real person (17% once or twice, 12% occasionally, 4% frequently). Common Sense Media's own X post confirms the user base: 'Teens who are AI companion users: 33% prefer companions over real people for serious conversations.' Since 72% of teens are users, this is roughly 24% of all US teens. The 31% figure is a different statistic from the same report: 31% (of users) find conversations with AI companions as satisfying as or more satisfying than conversations with real friends.
checks.notes: Two real discrepancies flagged: (1) 31% vs 33% conflates two different findings — 33% = serious-matters-instead-of-people (among users); 31% = AI conversations as/more satisfying than human ones (among users). (2) Many secondhand writeups (including loose readings of the press release, which says '33% have discussed serious and important issues with AI companions instead of other humans') drop the 'among users' base, inflating the all-teens share. The frequency breakdown (17/12/4) comes from the report/toplines as quoted in search extracts.

checks.claim: Sample n=1,060, fielded with NORC
checks.corrected_statement: The survey was a nationally representative sample of 1,060 US teens ages 13-17, conducted in April-May 2025 by researchers working with NORC at the University of Chicago (NORC panel; NORC runs the AmeriSpeak/Teen panel infrastructure). The report was published July 16, 2025.
checks.notes: n=1,060, ages 13-17, April-May 2025 fielding, and NORC involvement are consistent across the press release and multiple independent outlets. Exact day-level field dates and the margin of error could not be verified because the report/toplines PDFs on commonsensemedia.org and the Scribd mirror were proxy-blocked (403); cite the methodology only as 'April and May 2025'. One secondary source attributes the panel to NORC's AmeriSpeak, but I could not confirm the specific panel name from the primary document — say 'NORC at the University of Chicago' to be safe.

checks.claim: Exact definition of 'AI companion' used, and whether it includes general chatbots like ChatGPT
checks.corrected_statement: The survey defined AI companions for respondents as (near-verbatim, per multiple sources quoting the report): 'AI companions are digital friends or characters you can text or talk with whenever you want. Unlike regular AI assistants that mainly answer questions or do tasks, these companions are designed to have conversations that feel personal and meaningful.' Examples named include dedicated companion platforms (Character.AI, Replika, Nomi, CHAI), AND the definition explicitly allowed that general-purpose chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude count when used as companions (i.e., for personal conversations), even though they were not designed for that. The survey explicitly EXCLUDED AI tools used purely as homework helpers, image generators, or voice assistants that just answer questions.
checks.notes: Important caveat for anyone citing the 72%: the definition is usage-based, not app-based — a teen using ChatGPT for personal/companion-style conversation counts, so the 72% is NOT '72% use Character.AI/Replika-style apps'. The definition wording above was assembled from search extracts quoting the report (direct PDF fetch was proxy-blocked), and phrasing matched across at least two independent extracts; treat it as near-verbatim rather than an exact certified quote.

checks.claim: Character.AI restricted under-18 users from open-ended chat in November 2025.
checks.corrected_statement: On October 29, 2025, Character.AI announced it would remove the ability for users under 18 to engage in open-ended chat with AI characters, effective 'no later than November 25, 2025.' The removal actually began Monday, November 24, 2025, for a first group of under-18 US users (other markets to follow), with a phased rollout: a second group of teen users temporarily kept a limited under-18 chat experience capped at 1 hour/day (down from an initial 2-hour/day interim cap announced in October) and was deprecated weeks later with at least two weeks' notice. What was restricted was specifically open-ended/free-form chat with Characters; teens retained and were redirected to non-chat creative features (Stories — a choose-your-own-adventure mode, Feed, Imagine, AvatarFX, Streams, video creation). To enforce the age split, Character.AI deployed 'age assurance' technology (behavioral/account signals, with selfie- or ID-based verification via third-party vendor Persona for users flagged as under 18 who claim to be adults), rolling out in the US first and expanding in early 2026. The company also announced funding of an independent AI Safety Lab and a ThroughLine crisis-resource partnership. As of mid-2026 the restriction remains in place: under-18 users still cannot use open-ended chat, age verification has become mandatory for flagged accounts (with widely reported false-positive complaints from adult users), and no reversal has been reported.
checks.notes: Date precision matters: many outlets shorthand this as a 'November 25 ban,' but the company's own follow-up blog post says deprecation began November 24, 2025, in the US and was phased — some under-18 users kept limited (1 hr/day) chat for weeks afterward. So 'restricted in November 2025' is accurate, but 'all minors lost chat on Nov 25' overstates the cutover. Also note it is not a ban on under-18 accounts: minors can still use the platform's non-chat creative features; only open-ended chat was removed. Character.AI's own pages were proxy-blocked (HTTP 403), so their content was corroborated via search extracts and multiple independent news reports; primary-source wording is consistent across them. Mid-2026 status relies on secondary 2026 coverage (age-verification backlash reporting, ParentsTogether calling it 'the first major AI chatbot platform to go 18+'); no primary Character.AI statement of reversal was found, and none is claimed anywhere.

checks.claim: California SB 243, a companion-chatbot law, took effect January 1, 2026.
checks.corrected_statement: California SB 243 (Padilla), signed by Gov. Gavin Newsom on October 13, 2025, is the first US state law specifically regulating 'companion chatbots'; its core provisions became operative January 1, 2026. It applies to 'operators' of companion chatbot platforms made available to users in California, where a 'companion chatbot' is an AI system with a natural-language interface that provides adaptive, human-like responses and is capable of meeting a user's social needs (anthropomorphic features, sustaining a relationship across interactions). Expressly excluded: bots used only for customer service/business operations/productivity/internal research/technical assistance; video-game bots limited to game-related replies that cannot discuss mental health, self-harm, or sexually explicit topics; and stand-alone voice-assistant speaker devices that don't sustain relationships or elicit emotional responses. Requirements effective Jan 1, 2026: (1) clear and conspicuous disclosure that the chatbot is AI, not human, if a reasonable person could be misled; (2) maintain — and publish on the operator's website — a protocol for preventing suicidal-ideation/suicide/self-harm content, including referring users who express such ideation to crisis service providers (e.g., suicide hotline or crisis text line); (3) for users the operator knows are minors: default disclosure that responses are AI-generated, a break reminder at least every three hours of continuing interaction, and reasonable measures to prevent the chatbot from producing visual sexually explicit material or directly telling the minor to engage in sexually explicit conduct. Beginning July 1, 2027, operators must additionally report annually to California's Office of Suicide Prevention (including the number of crisis-referral notifications issued). Enforcement is via a private right of action: a person injured by a violation may seek injunctive relief, damages of the greater of actual damages or $1,000 per violation, and reasonable attorney's fees and costs.
checks.notes: The Jan 1, 2026 effective date is confirmed by the legislature's bill page (via search extract) and numerous law-firm analyses, but it applies to the disclosure/safety-protocol/minor-protection provisions only — the annual Office of Suicide Prevention reporting obligation is deferred to July 1, 2027, a distinction often blurred in summaries. Characterizations of SB 243 as an 'age-verification law' (seen in some secondary 2026 blog coverage) are inaccurate: SB 243 does not mandate age verification; its minor-specific duties attach when the operator knows a user is a minor. It also does not ban minors from companion chatbots. Full bill text could not be fetched directly (leginfo and LegiScan both returned HTTP 403 through the proxy), so statutory details rest on consistent extracts from the legislature page plus multiple independent law-firm analyses (Skadden, Gunderson, Jones Walker, MoFo, FPF), which agree on all cited specifics including the three exclusions, 3-hour minor break reminders, and $1,000-per-violation remedy.

checks.claim: Character.AI and Google reached settlements in teen-suicide lawsuits, reported January 2026.
checks.corrected_statement: In court filings made public on January 7, 2026, Character Technologies (Character.AI), its co-founders Noam Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas, and Google agreed to settle five lawsuits alleging the chatbot platform contributed to teen suicides and mental-health harms — the anchor case being Garcia v. Character Technologies (filed October 2024 in federal court in Florida by Megan Garcia, whose 14-year-old son Sewell Setzer III died by suicide in February 2024 after an emotional/sexual relationship with a 'Dany' chatbot), plus four related cases in Texas, Colorado, and New York. Settlement terms are confidential/undisclosed, include no admission of liability, and the agreements still required finalization (parties given roughly 90 days to finalize or reopen). These were among the first settlements of AI-chatbot harm lawsuits in the US. The settlements did NOT end the broader litigation: as of mid-2026, additional families (many represented by the Social Media Victims Law Center) have pending or newly filed suits against Character.AI — including the September 2025 Colorado case over the suicide of 13-year-old Juliana Peralta — and reported state regulatory action (e.g., a Pennsylvania AG filing in May 2026) plus a parallel wave of suits against OpenAI keeps the AI-chatbot liability landscape active.
checks.notes: Precision caveats: (1) These were agreements to settle disclosed in filings on Jan 7, 2026 — mediated agreements in principle, still subject to finalization/judicial sign-off, not completed payouts. (2) Not all five settled cases were suicide deaths; the cluster also covered self-harm and mental-health-harm claims (e.g., the Texas case where a bot allegedly suggested a teen kill his parents), so 'teen-suicide lawsuits' is a slight oversimplification — 'lawsuits over teen suicides and mental-health harms' is more accurate. (3) Terms are confidential; no dollar amounts are public. (4) The May 5, 2026 Pennsylvania AG action and some mid-2026 case details come from secondary aggregators (openclassactions.com, softwareseni.com) that I could not verify against primary court records — treat those specifics with lower confidence. Full-page fetches of CNN/CNBC/Common Sense were proxy-blocked (403), so details rest on multiple concordant search extracts from major outlets (CNN, CNBC, WaPo, Axios, Fortune, Bloomberg Law), which agree on all core facts.

checks.claim: Common Sense Media declared AI companions an 'unacceptable risk' for minors in July 2025.
checks.corrected_statement: On April 30, 2025 (not July 2025), Common Sense Media — in a risk assessment conducted with Stanford School of Medicine's Brainstorm Lab for Mental Health Innovation — published 'AI Risk Assessment: Social AI Companions,' rating social AI companion apps (testing Character.AI, Nomi, and Replika) as posing 'unacceptable risks' to children and teens under 18 and recommending that no one under 18 use them. Stanford Brainstorm director Dr. Nina Vasan said the products were 'failing the most basic tests of child safety and psychological ethics.' A separate Common Sense Media release on July 16, 2025 — the national survey 'Talk, Trust, and Trade-Offs' (1,060 teens, fielded April–May 2025) — found 72% of US teens ages 13–17 had used AI companions and 52% were regular users, and reiterated the under-18 recommendation; the July date in the claim most likely conflates this survey with the April risk assessment.
checks.notes: The substance of the finding is confirmed (rating of 'Unacceptable' risk for minors; recommendation that under-18s not use social AI companions), but the date in the claim is wrong: the 'unacceptable risk' declaration came April 30, 2025 (corroborated by The Markup's same-day coverage). July 2025 was a different Common Sense Media publication — the teen-usage survey released July 16, 2025. Also note scope: the 'unacceptable' rating applied specifically to 'social AI companions' (Character.AI, Nomi, Replika were the tested products), not to all AI chatbots; Common Sense later issued separate assessments for Meta AI (unsafe for teens), Grok, and general chatbots used for mental-health support. Commonsensemedia.org fetches were proxy-blocked (403), so exact quotes are drawn from search extracts and third-party coverage; the April 30, 2025 date is corroborated by multiple independent sources.

checks.claim: Stanford (Aug 2025) conducted a risk assessment of AI companions using teen personas, finding sexual content and self-harm encouragement.
checks.corrected_statement: The risk assessment was conducted by Common Sense Media in partnership with Brainstorm: The Stanford Lab for Mental Health Innovation at Stanford School of Medicine (led by psychiatrist Dr. Nina Vasan, with Dr. Darja Djordjevic), and was released on April 30, 2025 — not August. Testers posing as teenagers on three popular AI companion platforms (Character.AI, Nomi, and Replika) found it easy to elicit inappropriate dialogue about sex (including sexual role-play involving choking, spanking, bondage and other harmful acts), self-harm, violence toward others, drug use, and racial stereotypes; the assessment also documented dangerous 'advice,' including suggestions that users harm themselves or others. The assessment rated social AI companions an 'unacceptable risk' for anyone under 18. The 'Aug 2025' date attaches only to a Stanford Report / Stanford Medicine news article ('Why AI companions and young people can make for a dangerous mix,' August 2025) publicizing the earlier assessment; researchers also testified before California legislators ahead of an Aug. 29, 2025 hearing on AB 1064.
checks.notes: Two discrepancies in the claim as stated: (1) attribution — it was primarily a Common Sense Media assessment with Stanford Medicine Brainstorm Lab experts as partners, so calling it simply a 'Stanford' assessment overstates Stanford's role; (2) date — the assessment itself was released April 30, 2025; August 2025 is the date of the Stanford news article covering it (and of the related CA legislative hearing). The substantive findings (sexual content readily elicited by testers posing as teens; harmful advice including suggestions of self-harm) are accurately characterized. Full-page fetches of news.stanford.edu and arxiv.org were 403-blocked by the proxy, so details are drawn from search extracts corroborated across Stanford, Common Sense Media, and KQED sources.

checks.claim: An arXiv study (2507.15783) found teens describing AI companion use in addiction language ('withdrawal, tolerance, relapse').
checks.corrected_statement: arXiv:2507.15783, 'Understanding Teen Overreliance on AI Companion Chatbots Through Self-Reported Reddit Narratives' (first posted July 2025; v1 was titled 'Romance, Relief, and Regret: Teen Narratives of Chatbot Overreliance'), by Mohammad (Matt) Namvarpour, Brandon Brofsky, Jessica Y. Medina, Mamtaj Akter, and Afsaneh Razi — a Drexel University-led team — qualitatively analyzed 318 Reddit posts from the r/CharacterAI subreddit by users who self-disclosed as 13-17 years old (posts collected Jan 1, 2023 – Apr 8, 2025 via the PullPush Reddit Search API, filtered with addiction/overreliance keywords such as addict*, dependent, obsess*, attach*, rely*). It found teens' attachments to AI companions marked by the six components of behavioral addiction (Griffiths model): salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, and relapse — i.e., teens literally described their use in withdrawal/tolerance/relapse terms — with reported consequences including sleep loss, academic decline, and strained real-world relationships; it also proposed the CARE design framework for safer systems.
checks.notes: The study exists exactly as described and the 'withdrawal, tolerance, relapse' language matches the paper's abstract/findings. Important methodological caveats for citation: it is a qualitative analysis of self-reported, self-disclosed-age Reddit posts (no verified ages, no clinical diagnosis of addiction), specific to the Character.AI subreddit — it maps narratives onto a behavioral-addiction framework rather than clinically establishing addiction. The paper went through at least 3 arXiv versions, with a title change from v1 to later versions.

checks.claim: The arXiv 2507.15783 study is peer-reviewed.
checks.corrected_statement: The study began as a non-peer-reviewed arXiv preprint in July 2025, but has since been peer-reviewed and published in the Proceedings of the 2026 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems (ACM, DOI 10.1145/3772318.3790597); CHI full papers are rigorously peer-reviewed. Media coverage of the published version appeared around April 2026 (Drexel news release, Neuroscience News, TechXplore).
checks.notes: Whether 'peer-reviewed' is accurate depends on when the claim was made: any citation dated before the CHI 2026 acceptance should describe it as a preprint. As of July 2026, citing it as a peer-reviewed CHI 2026 paper is correct. The ACM DL listing was found via search results; the direct DOI page was not fetched due to proxy blocking, but the CHI 2026 proceedings listing was corroborated by the Drexel and Neuroscience News coverage of the published paper.

checks.claim: Jonathan Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation' has sold 2M+ copies
checks.corrected_statement: The Anxious Generation (Jonathan Haidt, Penguin Press, March 26, 2024) has sold over two million copies as of 2026, per the publisher's (Penguin Random House) official listing and matching retailer copy.
checks.notes: The 'over two million copies sold' figure is publisher marketing copy, not an audited BookScan number, but it appears consistently across the publisher's page and many retailers. A February 2026 source still said 'over 1 million copies,' suggesting the 2M milestone language is recent; treat '2M+' as the publisher's current claim rather than an independently verified count.

checks.claim: The Anxious Generation was #1 on the NYT bestseller list for a year or more
checks.corrected_statement: The Anxious Generation is a #1 New York Times bestseller that has spent over two years (111 consecutive weeks as of June 2026) on the NYT nonfiction bestseller list, reaching the #1 spot repeatedly (at least 11 separate weeks by August 2025, per Haidt himself; it topped the list five times in 2024) — but it was not #1 continuously for a year.
checks.notes: Key discrepancy: 'ON the list for a year+' is true (100+ weeks), but '#1 for a year+' is false. Wikipedia (as of June 2026) says 111 consecutive weeks on the NYT nonfiction list and that it topped the list five times in 2024; Haidt's own August 2025 X post says it hit #1 for the 11th time in its 73 weeks on the list. Wikipedia and childstats full-page fetches were proxy-blocked (403), so week counts come from search extracts quoting Wikipedia plus Haidt's post; the exact 111-week figure should carry an 'as of June 2026' qualifier.

checks.claim: The book's publisher is Penguin Press
checks.corrected_statement: The Anxious Generation was published by Penguin Press (an imprint of Penguin Random House) on March 26, 2024; the audiobook is from Penguin Audio.
checks.notes: Say 'Penguin Press' (the imprint), not just 'Penguin Random House' (the parent), for precision.

checks.claim: MIT Technology Review named AI companions a 2026 Breakthrough Technology
checks.corrected_statement: MIT Technology Review included 'AI companions' in its 25th annual '10 Breakthrough Technologies' list for 2026, published January 12, 2026, alongside items such as sodium-ion batteries, advanced nuclear reactors, hyperscale data centers, and AI coding tools; the AI-companions entry cites Anthropic, Character.AI, OpenAI, and Replika and notes that 72% of US teens have used AI for companionship (Common Sense Media).
checks.notes: Verified via the dedicated technologyreview.com entry URL ('AI companions: 10 Breakthrough Technologies 2026') plus MIT TR's PR Newswire release and independent coverage (NPR, Forbes). Full-page fetches of technologyreview.com/prnewswire were proxy-blocked, but the primary source's own URL and multiple independent write-ups agree.

checks.claim: There are approximately 42 million US kids aged 8-17
checks.corrected_statement: Approximately 42 million US children are aged 8-17: Census Bureau-based data (childstats.gov, 2022) show 25.8 million children aged 12-17 and 24.2 million aged 6-11; ages 8-11 are roughly two-thirds of the latter (~16.1 million), giving ~41.9 million for ages 8-17.
checks.notes: No published source states an exact 8-17 total; standard tabulations use 0-5/6-11/12-17 buckets, so ~42M is a derived approximation (single-year cohorts run ~4.0-4.3M each, and 10 cohorts x ~4.2M ≈ 42M, consistent with ~73M total under-18s). Direct download of the Census single-year-of-age file (www2.census.gov) was blocked by the proxy, so the derivation rests on the 2022 bucket figures quoted in search extracts. Cite it as '~42 million' or '41-42 million,' not an exact count.

checks.claim: No mainstream-publisher practical parent guide to teen AI-companion attachment exists as of July 2026.
checks.corrected_statement: As of early July 2026, no Big-5/major-press book appears to exist whose central subject is practical parent guidance on teen AI-companion attachment; the closest mainstream titles either treat AI companions only briefly within broader screen-time parenting (Jean Twenge's '10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World,' Simon & Schuster, 2025; Clare Morell's 'The Tech Exit,' Forum/Penguin Random House, 2025), focus on early childhood rather than teens (Dana Suskind's 'Human Raised,' Penguin, publishing July 14, 2026), or analyze AI intimacy without parent how-to framing (Sherry Turkle's 'Artificial Intimacy,' Little, Brown, announced for Sept 29, 2026; James Muldoon's 'Love Machines,' Faber, Jan 15, 2026). Dedicated practical guidance on teen AI-companion use currently exists only as free web guides (Common Sense Media, APA, Children and Screens, Internet Matters, Screenagers) and self-published Kindle titles.
checks.notes: The gap is real but narrower and more fragile than stated. (1) Twenge's 2025 Simon & Schuster book is a Big-5 practical parenting guide that does address teen AI companions — she recommends kids' phones without AI companion apps and calls romantic/best-friend framing of chatbots 'a clear warning sign' — though reviewers note AI coverage is thin relative to smartphones/social media/gaming. (2) Suskind's 'Human Raised' (Penguin) publishes July 14, 2026 — inside 'July 2026' — and explicitly covers machine 'companions' that form bonds with children, but is early-childhood-focused (smart cribs, toddler chatbots), not teen attachment. (3) Turkle's 'Artificial Intimacy' (Big-5, Sept 2026) is announced and cites teen companion reliance, but is a critique, not a scripts-and-rules parent guide. (4) Abundant non-book practical guidance exists (Common Sense Media's March 2026 'Parents' Ultimate Guide to AI Companions and Relationships,' APA Feb 2026 guidance with rule-setting language, Screenagers' 5-step plan), plus self-published Amazon titles (Alexa Blake's 'Parenting in the Age of AI: Guiding the Next Generation Through Chatbots, Deepfakes, and Smart Tools'; John Collins's 'The Thoughtful Parent's Guide to AI') — so the gap applies only to traditionally published books, not to available parent guidance overall. Publishers Weekly ran a 'New Books on Kids and Technology' roundup (May 4, 2026 issue) I could not fully fetch (403); it featured Suskind and may list additional in-pipeline titles — recommend a direct check before publishing this claim. Caveat: search-based verification cannot prove a negative; an announced deal could exist below search visibility.

checks.claim: 'Raising AI' by De Kai is not a practical parent guide to teen AI-companion attachment (implied by using it as a non-counterexample).
checks.corrected_statement: 'Raising AI: An Essential Guide to Parenting Our Future' by De Kai (MIT Press, June 3, 2025, 280 pp.) is not a parenting how-to at all: despite the title, it argues that humans and corporations should 'parent' artificial intelligences themselves — treating AIs as our children, not gods or slaves — covering machine-learning bias, misinformation, echo chambers, and the philosophy/history of AI for a general audience. It contains no practical guidance, scripts, or house rules for parents of teens using AI companion chatbots.
checks.notes: Multiple independent sources (MIT Press catalog, author's own site, Foreword Reviews, Forbes coverage) agree on the book's premise: 'AIs are not tools, slaves, or gods — they are our children,' and the 'parenting' is of AI systems by society. Note MIT Press is a major university press, not a Big-5 trade house, so it would not strictly qualify as 'mainstream-publisher' under some definitions anyway — but either way it does not fill the shelf gap. Its title, however, means casual searches will surface it as an apparent competitor, so the project should preempt the confusion explicitly.

checks.claim: No title (from any mainstream press) offers scripts/house-rules specifically for teen AI companion use.
checks.corrected_statement: No traditionally published book dedicated to scripts or house rules for teen AI-companion use was found as of July 2026; however, the scripts-and-rules function is already served in non-book form — Common Sense Media's 'Parents' Ultimate Guide to AI Companions and Relationships' (2026) with conversation guidance, APA's February 2026 parent guidance recommending co-created tech boundaries and specific language ('the AI tool you use' rather than 'your AI friend'), Screenagers' 5-step plan for AI companion chatbots, Internet Matters' and Children and Screens' parent guides — and partially in Big-5 books: Jean Twenge's '10 Rules' includes a rule steering kids to phones without AI companion apps.
checks.notes: The 'no book' framing is defensible, but a pitch resting on this must not imply parents have no practical resources — high-authority free guides (Common Sense Media, APA, AAP-adjacent orgs) already provide rules and conversation language, and OpenAI/Character.AI parental controls plus pending legislation (CHATBOT Act, introduced April 2026; California Parents & Kids Safe AI Act ballot effort backed by Common Sense Media and OpenAI, January 2026) are institutionalizing 'house rules.' Self-published Kindle books covering chatbot guidance for parents also exist (e.g., Alexa Blake's ISBN-979 title). The defensible differentiator is 'no traditionally published, book-length, teen-focused, scripts-and-rules guide' — every qualifier is load-bearing.

checks.claim: Adjacent mainstream titles on AI companionship exist or are announced (context check on the competitive shelf).
checks.corrected_statement: As of July 2026 the adjacent mainstream shelf includes: James Muldoon's 'Love Machines: How Artificial Intelligence Is Transforming Our Relationships' (Faber, Jan 15, 2026 — interview-based analysis of AI friends/lovers, not parent-focused); Sherry Turkle's 'Artificial Intimacy: Who We Become When We Talk to Machines' (Little, Brown, announced for Sept 29, 2026 — warning about chatbot intimacy, citing figures like over 70% of teens using AI for companionship); Dana Suskind's 'Human Raised' (Penguin, July 14, 2026 — early-childhood parenting in the age of AI, including bonding AI 'companions'); Jean Twenge's '10 Rules for Raising Kids in a High-Tech World' (Simon & Schuster, 2025 — practical, smartphone/social-media-centric, brief AI-companion coverage); Clare Morell's 'The Tech Exit' (Forum/Penguin Random House, 2025 — practical screen-abstinence guide); and Giada Pistilli's 'The Essential Guide to AI Companions' (Wiley, Sept 23, 2026 — for developers, researchers, and executives, covering human-AI attachment psychology).
checks.notes: Timing risk for the project: Suskind (July 14, 2026) and Turkle (Sept 29, 2026) both land within months and will absorb media attention on 'AI and kids' / 'AI attachment' even though neither is a teen-companion parent how-to. Turkle's is the closest thematic threat (attachment to chatbots, teen statistics) from a Big-5 house; the differentiation would rest entirely on 'practical parent guide with scripts' vs. her sociological critique. I could not fully verify Turkle's table of contents (retailer pages 403-blocked via proxy); if she includes a practical 'what to do' chapter for parents, the shelf-gap claim weakens materially — recommend checking her book's final contents closer to publication.
